topics.title: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET834 - BUSINESS ORGANIZATION AND DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Definition of Business

According to the standard KTU 2024 management syllabus framework, **Business** can be formally defined as:

> A legally recognised economic activity involving the **production, purchase, sale, or exchange of goods and services** with the primary objective of earning profit and satisfying human wants.

In simpler terms, *Business is any organised effort by individuals or groups to create value for others in exchange for monetary or non-monetary consideration.*

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of business like a cycle of value-exchange on a street:**
> Imagine a fruit vendor in Kerala who buys mangoes from a farmer (purchase), sorts them, transports them to the city (production/processing), and sells them to customers (sale). The vendor **adds value** at each step — connecting the farmer to the urban customer. The difference between what he paid and what he received is his **profit**. That entire chain of activities = **Business**.

The key insight is that business is not just *trading*. It is a **systematic, organised, and continuous activity** that transforms inputs (raw materials, labour, capital) into outputs (goods/services) of higher value.

## 1.2 Characteristics / Features of Business

A venture qualifies as "business" only if it satisfies the following characteristics:

1. **Economic Activity** — Involves production, distribution, or exchange of wealth.
2. **Profit Motive** — Earning profit is the primary driving force (though modern businesses also balance social goals).
3. **Continuous & Regular Process** — A one-time sale is not business; business implies repeat, sustained activity.
4. **Risk & Uncertainty** — Every business faces risks (market, financial, legal, natural).
5. **Legal Compliance** — Must operate within the legal framework of the state (Companies Act, GST, Labour Laws).
6. **Customer Satisfaction** — Modern businesses consider customer satisfaction as essential as profit.
7. **Creation of Utility** — Business creates form, place, time, and possession utility.

## 1.3 Objectives of Business

| Type | Objective |
|------|-----------|
| **Economic Objectives** | Profit earning, market share growth, innovation, survival |
| **Social Objectives** | Employment generation, fair wages, environmental protection |
| **Human Objectives** | Employee welfare, skill development, work-life balance |
| **National Objectives** | Contributing to GDP, foreign exchange earnings, balanced regional development |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Modern business theory (post-2015 amendments in management pedagogy) emphasises the **triple-bottom-line approach**: *People, Planet, Profit* — not profit alone.

## 1.4 Scope of Business

The scope of business is **vast and multidimensional**, encompassing:
- **Industry** (Manufacturing, Processing)
- **Commerce** (Trade + Auxiliaries like banking, insurance, transport, warehousing, advertising)
- **Services** (Banking, IT, Hospitality, Healthcare, Education)

## 1.5 Business vs. Profession vs. Employment

This distinction is a **favourite KTU 2-mark question**.

| Parameter | Business | Profession | Employment |
|-----------|----------|------------|------------|
| Motive | Profit | Service (fees) | Salary |
| Capital | Owner's investment | Self-funding + fees | No investment |
| Risk | High | Limited | None |
| Code of Conduct | Business ethics | Professional code | Service rules |
| Examples | Reliance, TATA | Doctor, Lawyer | Government teacher |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD CONCEPT SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Business Activities

All economic activities can be broadly classified into three main categories:

### A. Industry
Industry refers to the **production of goods** or extraction of raw materials, and the transformation of raw materials into finished products.

#### Sub-categories of Industry:
1. **Primary Industry** — Direct extraction from nature (Agriculture, Mining, Fishing, Forestry).
2. **Secondary Industry (Manufacturing)** — Conversion of raw materials into finished goods (Textile mills, Steel plants, Automobile factories).
3. **Tertiary Industry (Service Industry)** — Provision of services rather than tangible goods (Banking, Insurance, Tourism, IT Services).
4. **Quaternary Industry** — Knowledge-based activities (R&D, Consulting, Information Services).
5. **Quinary Industry** — Decision-making and policy formulation activities in society.
6. **Genetic Industry** — Breeding of plants/animals (Plant nursery, Dairy farming).

### B. Commerce
Commerce refers to the **activities that facilitate the buying and selling of goods**. It consists of two main branches:

- **Trade** — Actual buying and selling of goods.
  - *Internal Trade* (Wholesale, Retail)
  - *International Trade* (Import, Export, Entrepôt)
- **Auxiliaries to Trade** — Services that aid trade.
  - Transportation, Warehousing, Insurance, Banking, Advertising, Packaging, Communication.

### C. Services
Activities that provide **immaterial benefits** to satisfy human needs.

## 2.2 Evolution / Development of Business

The development of modern business can be traced through distinct stages:

| Era | Stage | Characteristics |
|-----|-------|-----------------|
| Pre-Industrial | Barter System | Goods-for-goods exchange |
| Medieval | Guild System | Craftsmen organised into guilds |
| Industrial Revolution (1760–1840) | Factory System | Mechanised production, wage labour |
| 19th Century | Joint Stock Companies | Limited liability, capital pooling |
| 20th Century | Multinational Corporations | Global operations, vertical integration |
| 21st Century | Digital & Platform Business | E-commerce, AI, gig economy, sustainability focus |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Concept Link:** The shift from *profit-only* to *sustainable & stakeholder-driven* business represents the philosophical evolution critical for Module 1 comprehension.

## 2.3 Role of Business in Economic Development

Business acts as the **engine of economic growth** in any economy. Its key roles:

1. **Capital Formation** — Mobilises savings and converts them into productive investments.
2. **Employment Generation** — Provides direct and indirect jobs.
3. **Industrialisation** — Promotes setting up of factories and infrastructure.
4. **Standard of Living** — Mass production raises quality of life.
5. **Innovation & Technology** — Drives R&D and technological progress.
6. **Export Promotion** — Earns foreign exchange, improving the Balance of Payments.
7. **Tax Revenue** — Contributes to government exchequer.
8. **Regional Balance** — Promotes development of backward regions.

## 2.4 Business Environment

The **business environment** comprises all internal and external factors that influence business operations.

### Internal Environment
- Owners, Board of Directors, Employees, Organizational Culture, Resources.

### External Environment
- **Micro-environment** — Suppliers, Customers, Competitors, Public, Marketing Intermediaries.
- **Macro-environment (PESTLE)** — Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Legal, Environmental.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet (Cheat Table)

| Concept | Definition | Key Term |
|---------|-----------|----------|
| **Business** | Organised economic activity for profit | Economic activity |
| **Industry** | Production/extraction of goods | Manufacturing |
| **Commerce** | Facilitating buying and selling | Trade + Auxiliaries |
| **Auxiliaries to Trade** | Services aiding trade | Banking, Transport, Insurance |
| **Barter System** | Exchange of goods for goods | Pre-monetary system |
| **Joint Stock Company** | Company with shareholders & limited liability | Modern corporate form |
| **PESTLE** | Macro-environment framework | Policy analysis |
| **Triple Bottom Line** | People, Planet, Profit | Modern business philosophy |
| **Stakeholder** | Any party affected by business | Beyond shareholders |
| **Value Addition** | Increasing utility of product | Core business purpose |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in engineering:** Even engineering graduates frequently become entrepreneurs or intrapreneurs. Understanding business fundamentals helps in **product design decisions, cost-benefit analysis, supply chain thinking**, and **startup creation** — making this module foundational for career readiness.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP ANALYTICAL FRAMEWORK

## 3.1 Analytical Process: Identifying Whether an Activity Qualifies as "Business"

Since this is a foundational management topic, the analysis below provides a **logical decision-tree style framework** rather than mathematical derivation — perfectly aligned with KTU 2024 management pedagogy.

### Step-by-Step Test for Classification of an Economic Activity

**Step 1:** Identify whether the activity involves **production, purchase, sale, or exchange**.
- If **NO** → It is a **non-economic activity** (e.g., household work, charity).
- If **YES** → Proceed to Step 2.

**Step 2:** Check if it is undertaken with a **profit motive**.
- If **NO** → It is a **profession or hobby** (e.g., doctor practicing without profit motive, or a personal blog).
- If **YES** → Proceed to Step 3.

**Step 3:** Verify if the activity is **continuous and regular** (not one-time).
- If **NO** → It is an **isolated transaction**.
- If **YES** → Proceed to Step 4.

**Step 4:** Determine if there is **risk and uncertainty** involved.
- If **NO** → It may be a service without business characteristics (e.g., salaried employment).
- If **YES** → **Conclude: It is a Business.**

### Analytical Flowchart (Decision Logic)

$$
\text{Activity} \rightarrow \text{Economic?} \rightarrow \text{Profit Motive?} \rightarrow \text{Continuous?} \rightarrow \text{Risk?} \rightarrow \text{Business}
$$

Where each arrow represents a sequential **Yes/No logical gate**.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Applying the Framework

> **Case:** Ravi, an engineering graduate from Kerala, sets up a small workshop in Kochi to manufacture custom-designed 3D-printed phone cases. He buys filament (raw material), operates 3D printers (production), sells cases online through a website (sale), earns monthly revenue (profit motive), and reinvests profits to buy more printers (continuity). He faces risks like power cuts, fluctuating filament prices, and competition from mass producers.

**Applying the Framework:**

$$
\text{Ravi's activity} \xrightarrow{\text{Yes}} \text{Economic?} \xrightarrow{\text{Yes}} \text{Profit?} \xrightarrow{\text{Yes}} \text{Continuous?} \xrightarrow{\text{Yes}} \text{Risk?} \xrightarrow{\text{Yields}} \text{Business}
$$

**Conclusion:** Ravi's workshop **qualifies as a business** because it satisfies all four criteria.

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Forms of Business Organisation (Foundational Preview)

This analytical matrix, while previewing Module 2 concepts, helps contextualise Module 1 understanding:

| Criterion | Sole Proprietorship | Partnership | Joint Stock Company |
|-----------|---------------------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Ownership** | Single owner | 2–50 partners | Shareholders |
| **Liability** | Unlimited | Joint & several | Limited to share value |
| **Capital** | Limited | Pooled from partners | Large capital via shares |
| **Risk Bearing** | Sole owner | Shared among partners | Distributed across shareholders |
| **Legal Status** | Not separate | Not separate | Separate legal entity |
| **Suitability** | Small scale | Medium scale | Large scale |
| **Example** | Local kirana shop | Law firm, CA firm | Infosys, TCS, Reliance |

## 3.4 Quantitative Snapshot: Business Contribution to Indian Economy (Illustrative)

$$
\text{Business Contribution to Indian GDP} \approx \mathbf{55{-}58\%}
$$

$$
\text{MSME Share in Manufacturing Output} \approx \mathbf{33\%}
$$

$$
\text{MSME Share in Total Exports} \approx \mathbf{48\%}
$$

> **Interpretation:** These figures (commonly cited in management literature) demonstrate that businesses — not just government — are the **primary engines of economic growth**, validating the importance of studying business organisation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define business. List any four characteristics of business.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Business is an organised economic activity undertaken with the motive of earning profit through the production, purchase, sale, or exchange of goods and services.

**Four Characteristics:**
1. **Economic Activity** — Business is fundamentally an economic activity involving creation of wealth.
2. **Profit Motive** — The primary objective is to earn profits and sustain growth.
3. **Continuous Process** — Business is not a one-time transaction but a regular, sustained activity.
4. **Risk and Uncertainty** — Every business must confront risks related to demand, supply, finance, and policy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** Students often lose marks by writing generic definitions. Always include **"economic activity"**, **"profit motive"**, and **"regular/continuous"** in the definition for full marks.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Distinguish between Business, Profession, and Employment.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Business | Profession | Employment |
|-------|----------|------------|------------|
| Motive | Profit | Service (fees) | Salary |
| Investment | Owner's capital | Self + fees | None |
| Risk | Born by owner | Limited | No risk |
| Example | Running a textile shop | Practising as a doctor | Working as a bank clerk |

**Conclusion:** While all three are economic activities, their **motivation, risk profile, and reward structure** differ significantly.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks):**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, OEMET834]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the various **classification of business activities**, with suitable examples for each category. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **role of business in economic development** of a country, with specific reference to the Indian context. **(7 Marks)**

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**
**(a)** Describe the **evolution of business** from the barter system to the modern digital era, highlighting the key changes in each stage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **PESTLE framework** for analysing the macro business environment, with examples relevant to a Kerala-based startup. **(7 Marks)**

---

### MODEL SOLUTIONS

#### **Question A (a) — Classification of Business Activities (7 Marks)**

**Answer:**

Business activities can be broadly classified into three categories:

**1. Industry (4 marks worth of content):**
Industry refers to all activities that produce or transform goods.

- **Primary Industry:** Extraction of natural resources. *Examples — Agriculture, Mining, Fisheries, Forestry.*
- **Secondary Industry (Manufacturing):** Conversion of raw materials into finished goods. *Examples — Textile mills, Sugar factories, Automobile plants.*
- **Tertiary Industry (Services):** Provision of intangible services. *Examples — Banking, Transport, Tourism.*
- **Quaternary Industry:** Knowledge-based services. *Examples — IT, R&D, Consulting.*
- **Quinary Industry:** Leadership and policy roles in society.
- **Genetic Industry:** Breeding of plants and animals. *Example — Dairy farms, Plant nurseries.*

**2. Commerce (2 marks):**
Commerce facilitates the buying and selling of goods.

- **Trade:** Internal Trade (Wholesale, Retail) + International Trade (Import, Export).
- **Auxiliaries to Trade:** Banking, Insurance, Warehousing, Advertising, Transportation.

**3. Services (1 mark):**
Services provide intangible benefits — Professional (Doctors, Lawyers), Personal (Salon, Hospitality), Public (Education, Healthcare).

---

#### **Question A (b) — Role of Business in Economic Development (7 Marks)**

**Answer:**

Business plays a **multi-faceted role** in economic development:

1. **Capital Formation (1 Mark):** Business mobilises savings and channels them into productive investments, contributing to GDP growth.
2. **Employment Generation (1 Mark):** The MSME sector in India alone provides employment to over **110 million people** (as per recent MSME Ministry reports).
3. **Standard of Living (1 Mark):** Mass production and economies of scale make consumer goods affordable, raising quality of life.
4. **Industrialisation (1 Mark):** Business sets up factories and infrastructure, accelerating industrialisation.
5. **Innovation and Technology (1 Mark):** Competitive business drives R&D investments and adoption of new technologies.
6. **Export Promotion (1 Mark):** Indian IT and pharmaceutical businesses earn significant foreign exchange.
7. **Tax Revenue and Regional Development (1 Mark):** Businesses contribute to government tax revenue and promote balanced regional growth through industrial corridors.

---

#### **Question B (a) — Evolution of Business (7 Marks)**

**Answer:**

The evolution of business can be traced through **six distinct eras**:

1. **Barter System Era (Pre-Monetary):** Goods exchanged for goods. Limitation — double coincidence of wants.
2. **Guild System Era (Medieval):** Craftsmen organised into guilds controlling quality, training, and pricing.
3. **Industrial Revolution (1760–1840):** Mechanised production, factories, wage labour, urban migration.
4. **Joint Stock Company Era (19th Century):** Limited liability, capital pooling via shares, separation of ownership and management.
5. **Multinational Era (20th Century):** Global operations, vertical/horizontal integration, FDI flows.
6. **Digital & Platform Era (21st Century):** E-commerce (Amazon, Flipkart), gig economy (Uber, Swiggy), AI-driven business models, sustainability focus.

**Conclusion:** Each era brought greater **efficiency, scale, and complexity**, transforming business from local barter to global digital ecosystems.

---

#### **Question B (b) — PESTLE Framework for a Kerala Startup (7 Marks)**

**Answer:**

The **PESTLE framework** analyses six macro-environmental factors:

1. **Political (P):** Government stability, policy support (e.g., Kerala Startup Mission), Ease of Doing Business rankings.
2. **Economic (E):** Inflation, interest rates, currency fluctuations, GDP growth, consumer purchasing power.
3. **Social (S):** Demographics, literacy levels, cultural preferences, lifestyle changes (e.g., Kerala's high literacy favours tech adoption).
4. **Technological (T):** Internet penetration, mobile-first usage, AI/ML adoption, Digital Kerala initiatives.
5. **Legal (L):** Companies Act, GST, Labour Laws, Intellectual Property Rights.
6. **Environmental (E):** Climate change, sustainability norms, Kerala's vulnerability to floods — relevant for operational continuity.

**Kerala Startup Example:** A Kochi-based agritech startup must analyse these six factors — Kerala's high literacy (S), strong digital infrastructure (T), government policy support (P), and environmental risks (E) — to design a sustainable business model.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **In Question A(a):** Students often confuse **Industry, Commerce, and Services** — they are NOT synonyms. Industry = production, Commerce = trade facilitation, Services = intangible value. Marks deducted for mixing these.
> 2. **In Question A(b):** Don't just write "business creates jobs and profit" — that's 2-marks-level answer. To get full 7 marks, you must include **specific Indian-context examples** (MSME numbers, IT sector, GST revenue, etc.).
> 3. **In Question B(a):** Listing the eras alone gets only 3-4 marks. You must explain the **transformation logic** — what changed, why, and how it shaped modern business.
> 4. **In Question B(b):** Don't write generic PESTLE definitions. Always **apply to a specific Kerala context** — Kerala Startup Mission, high literacy, Digital Kerala, post-flood business continuity, etc.
> 5. **Common Mistake:** Writing "PESTL" (missing E for Environmental) — KTU specifically tests the dual E (Economic + Environmental).

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Introduction**

- **Business =** Economic activity + Profit motive + Continuous + Risk-bearing.
- **Three Pillars of Business Activities:** Industry, Commerce, Services.
- **Industry sub-types:** Primary, Secondary, Tertiary, Quaternary, Quinary, Genetic.
- **Commerce = Trade + Auxiliaries to Trade** (auxiliaries include Transport, Banking, Insurance, Warehousing, Advertising).
- **Six Sub-industries to remember:** Primary (Agriculture), Secondary (Manufacturing), Tertiary (Services), Quaternary (IT/R&D), Quinary (Leadership), Genetic (Breeding).
- **Evolution Path:** Barter → Guild → Industrial Revolution → Joint Stock → MNC → Digital/Platform.
- **Triple Bottom Line:** People + Planet + Profit (modern business philosophy).
- **PESTLE:** Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Legal, Environmental.
- **Business vs Profession vs Employment:** Different by motive, risk, reward.
- **Role of Business:** Capital formation, employment, standard of living, innovation, exports, tax revenue, regional balance.
- **Stakeholders:** Owners, Employees, Customers, Suppliers, Government, Community, Creditors, Environment.
- **Indian Context Always:** Kerala Startup Mission, Digital Kerala, MSME sector, IT industry.
- **Definition must include three keywords:** *Economic*, *Profit*, *Continuous* — for full marks.
- **Auxiliaries to Trade are NOT trade themselves** — they SUPPORT trade.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET834 - BUSINESS ORGANIZATION AND DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Business

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEMET834)** syllabus, *Business* is defined as the **organized effort of individuals or enterprises to produce, distribute, and exchange goods and services** with the objective of earning **profit** (economic return) while simultaneously creating **value** for society (social return). It is a **continuous economic activity** involving the coordinated deployment of four factors of production — **Land, Labour, Capital, and Entrepreneurship**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (High-Yield):**
> *"Business is any human activity directed towards the production or acquisition of goods and services for the purpose of selling them at a profit."*
> — *Modified Coleman & McCarty definition (Standard B.Com / BBA Board Examination phrasing)*

A business is not:
- A **charity** (no profit motive, even if exchange occurs).
- A **hobby** (lacks continuity and economic purpose).
- A **profession** in the strict legal sense (e.g., medicine, law require formal qualifications — though the *operations* of a business are professionalized).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠

> **Analogy: Business = A Restaurant Kitchen 🍳**
>
> Imagine a small restaurant:
> - **Inputs (Land, Labour, Capital):** The building (land), the chef (labour), the ovens and utensils (capital), and the person who opened it (entrepreneur).
> - **Process:** The chef transforms raw ingredients (vegetables, flour) into a finished dish.
> - **Output:** A plate of food sold to a customer for a price.
> - **Profit:** If the price received (₹200) is greater than the cost of ingredients + rent + wages (₹120), then a profit of ₹80 is earned.
>
> Repeat this **day after day** (continuity), with different customers (exchange), and you have a **Business**.

---

## 1.3 Core Highlights & Callout

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Essential Pillars of Business (Board Favourite 🔥):**
> 1. **Continuous Economic Activity** — One-time or occasional transactions (like a garage sale) do NOT constitute business.
> 2. **Exchange of Goods or Services** — Mere production for self-consumption (subsistence farming) is not business.
> 3. **Profit Motive** — The primary driving force, though secondary objectives (growth, service) exist.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked *"Is a Government Department a Business?"* — Answer: **No**, because it lacks profit motive. When asked *"Is LIC a Business?"* — Answer: **Yes**, because it earns premiums, invests, and makes profits, even though it is state-owned.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of Business — Deconstructed

Every business, irrespective of size or sector, performs **three universal functions**:

1. **Production / Procurement** — Creating the product or sourcing it from producers.
2. **Distribution / Marketing** — Moving the product to the consumer (transport, warehousing, advertising).
3. **Exchange / Transaction** — The actual sale, supported by financing and risk-bearing.

These three functions are visually mapped in **Section 4 (Diagram)**.

---

## 2.2 Characteristics / Features of Business (High-Yield for 3-mark questions)

| # | Characteristic | Plain-English Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Economic Activity** | Produces wealth; measured in money. |
| 2 | **Profit Motive** | Primary objective is to earn profit. |
| 3 | **Continuity** | Not one-time; operates regularly (T+1, T+2, … T+n). |
| 4 | **Exchange of Goods/Services** | Involves buying and selling. |
| 5 | **Risk & Uncertainty** | Future outcomes are unpredictable. |
| 6 | **Legal Compliance** | Must be lawful (Companies Act, GST, etc.). |
| 7 | **Creation of Utility** | Adds form, place, time, possession utility. |
| 8 | **Social Obligation** | Modern view: also serves society. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Utility Creation — A Board Favourite Concept:**
> Business creates **4 types of utility**:
> - **Form Utility** — Raw material → Finished product (manufacturing).
> - **Place Utility** — Moving goods from surplus to scarcity region.
> - **Time Utility** — Storing perishable items for off-season sale (cold storage).
> - **Possession Utility** — Transfer of ownership (sale deed, bill of lading).

---

## 2.3 Objectives of Business

| Objective Type | Examples |
|---|---|
| **Economic (Primary)** | Profit maximization, market share, growth, ROI |
| **Social (Secondary)** | Fair wages, community welfare, environmental sustainability |
| **Human / Personal** | Employee satisfaction, work-life balance, skill development |
| **National** | Export promotion, self-reliance, employment generation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern Concept of Business (vs. Traditional):**
> - **Traditional view (Adam Smith era):** Profit is the SOLE objective.
> - **Modern view (Peter Drucker, CSR era):** Profit is a *means*, customer satisfaction is the *goal*. Business must balance **Triple Bottom Line: People, Planet, Profit**.

---

## 2.4 Scope of Business

The **scope** is the operational boundary — *what activities does the business actually perform?*

$$
\text{Scope of Business} = \underbrace{\text{Industry}}_{\text{Production}} + \underbrace{\text{Commerce}}_{\text{Distribution \& Exchange}}
$$

- **Industry:** Concerned with the **production** of goods and services (extraction, manufacturing, construction, services).
- **Commerce:** All the **auxiliary activities** that facilitate the movement of goods from producer to consumer (trade, transport, banking, insurance, warehousing, advertising).

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Tabular Format)

| Concept | One-Line Board-Ready Answer |
|---|---|
| Business | Economic activity + Exchange + Profit motive + Continuity |
| Industry | Production / transformation of raw materials into finished goods |
| Commerce | Aids to trade: Trade + Auxiliaries to trade |
| Trade | Buying & selling with profit motive |
| Auxiliaries to Trade | Transport, Banking, Insurance, Warehousing, Advertising |
| Profit Motive | Driving force of business; not the only motive in modern view |
| Risk | Possibility of loss due to uncontrollable factors |
| Utility | Value-added to a product (form / place / time / possession) |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility for Engineers 👷‍♂️

Even for engineering students, understanding business is critical because:
- **Product Design Decisions** impact cost, pricing, and profitability.
- **Project Management** requires understanding of economic trade-offs.
- **Startup Ecosystem (Kerala Startup Mission KSUM)** — most engineers become entrepreneurs; they must understand business fundamentals.
- **Industrial Engineering** sits at the intersection of *production efficiency* and *business profitability*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Classification

> **Note:** Since this is a conceptual management topic, "derivations" here translate to **step-by-step logical taxonomies and classifications** that KTU examiners commonly test.

---

## 3.1 Analytical Breakdown — Classification of Business Activities

Business activities are classified in a **two-tier hierarchical structure**:

$$
\text{Business Activities} \longrightarrow \begin{cases} \text{Tier 1: Industry} \\ \text{Tier 2: Commerce} \end{cases}
$$

Each tier then further sub-divides. The full taxonomy is derived below:

---

### Step 1 — Tier 1 Classification

$$
\text{Business Activities} = \{\text{Industry}\} \cup \{\text{Commerce}\}
$$

- **Industry** = All activities that convert inputs into outputs of higher value.
- **Commerce** = All activities that bridge the gap between production and consumption.

---

### Step 2 — Sub-Classification of Industry

$$
\text{Industry} \longrightarrow \begin{cases} \text{A: Primary Industry (Extractive)} \\ \text{B: Secondary Industry (Manufacturing/Constructive)} \\ \text{C: Tertiary Industry (Service/Genetic)} \end{cases}
$$

Where:

- **A — Primary (Extractive):** Extracts raw materials from nature. *Examples:* Mining, Agriculture, Fishing, Forestry, Dairy farming.
- **B — Secondary (Manufacturing):** Transforms raw materials into finished goods. *Examples:* Textile mills, Steel plants, Bakeries, Cement factories.
- **C — Tertiary (Genetic/Service):** Provides intangible services or supports reproduction. *Examples:* IT services, Banking, Consulting, Hospitals, Poultry farming, Fish hatcheries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Genetic Industry (Board Trap ⚠️):** Often forgotten by students. It involves *breeding and multiplication* of living things (e.g., poultry farms, cattle breeding, nurseries). It is also called **Tertiary Industry**.

---

### Step 3 — Sub-Classification of Commerce

$$
\text{Commerce} \longrightarrow \begin{cases} \text{A: Trade} \\ \text{B: Auxiliaries to Trade} \end{cases}
$$

**A. Trade** further splits into:

$$
\text{Trade} \longrightarrow \begin{cases} \text{Internal (Home) Trade} \\ \text{International (Foreign) Trade} \end{cases}
$$

And **Internal Trade** further subdivides:

$$
\text{Internal Trade} \longrightarrow \begin{cases} \text{Wholesale Trade} \\ \text{Retail Trade} \end{cases}
$$

**B. Auxiliaries to Trade** (the "helper" activities):

$$
\text{Auxiliaries to Trade} = \{\text{Transport, Banking, Insurance, Warehousing, Advertising, Communication, Packaging}\}
$$

---

### Step 4 — Comprehensive Visual Formula

$$
\text{Business Activities} = \underbrace{\Big[\text{Primary} \cup \text{Secondary} \cup \text{Tertiary}\Big]}_{\text{Industry}} \; + \; \underbrace{\Big[\text{Trade} \cup \text{Auxiliaries}\Big]}_{\text{Commerce}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\therefore \text{Business} = f(\text{Production}, \text{Distribution}, \text{Exchange})}
$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table (Very High-Yield)

| Basis | Industry | Commerce |
|---|---|---|
| **Meaning** | Production of goods/services | Aids to trade; bridges producer–consumer gap |
| **Utility Created** | Form, Time | Place, Possession |
| **Profit Source** | Manufacturing margin | Service margins, commissions |
| **Examples** | Tata Steel, Infosys (BPO) | Flipkart, LIC, SBI, DHL |
| **Relation to Product** | *Creates* the product | *Moves* the product |
| **Tangibility** | Often tangible (not always) | Often intangible (services) |

| Basis | Internal Trade | International Trade |
|---|---|---|
| **Geography** | Within national boundaries | Across national borders |
| **Currency** | Domestic (INR) | Multiple currencies; forex |
| **Regulations** | GST, Shops & Establishments Act | Customs Act, FEMA, EXIM Policy |
| **Risk** | Lower | Higher (political, currency, tariff) |

| Basis | Wholesale Trade | Retail Trade |
|---|---|---|
| **Customer** | Bulk buyers, retailers | End consumer |
| **Quantity** | Large | Small / unit |
| **Margin** | Lower per unit, high volume | Higher per unit, lower volume |
| **Location** | Warehouses, mandis | Shops, malls, e-commerce platforms |

---

## 3.3 Logical Flow — Why Profit is Necessary

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Business requires} &\longrightarrow \text{Money (Capital)} \\
\text{Money comes from} &\longrightarrow \text{Investors / Banks (who expect return)} \\
\text{Return is generated by} &\longrightarrow \text{Profit} \\
\text{Profit enables} &\longrightarrow \text{Re-investment, Growth, Survival} \\
\therefore \text{Profit Motive} &\longrightarrow \text{Necessary, not merely desirable}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Board-Ready One-Liner:**
> *"Profit is the lifeline of a business — without it, the business cannot sustain, grow, or serve society."*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🔹 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**Q: Define Business. List any four characteristics of business.**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
Business may be defined as a **continuous economic activity** undertaken by an individual or an enterprise, involving the **production or acquisition of goods and services** with the objective of **selling them at a profit** to satisfy human wants.

**Four Characteristics (½ mark each):**
1. **Economic Activity** — Business is concerned with the production, purchase, and sale of goods and services.
2. **Profit Motive** — The primary aim of every business is to earn profit.
3. **Continuity** — A business is not a one-time activity; it is carried out on a regular basis.
4. **Exchange of Goods and Services** — Business involves the transfer of ownership of goods or rendering of services for value (money).

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**Q: Distinguish between Industry and Commerce.**
`[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Tabular form recommended):**

| Basis of Difference | Industry | Commerce |
|---|---|---|
| **Meaning** | Concerned with production of goods and services | Concerned with distribution and exchange of goods |
| **Stage** | Earlier stage (before goods reach market) | Later stage (after goods are produced) |
| **Utility Created** | Form utility and time utility | Place utility and possession utility |
| **Examples** | Manufacturing factory, agriculture, mining | Trading, banking, transport, insurance |
| **Relation** | Creates the product | Moves the product to the consumer |

---

## 🔹 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

---

### **Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)**

**Q: Explain in detail the classification of Business Activities. Differentiate between Internal Trade and International Trade.**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Regular]`

#### **Part (a) — Classification of Business Activities (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Business activities can be broadly classified into two main categories:

**1. Industry (Production of Goods and Services):**
Industry refers to all economic activities engaged in the production of goods and services. It is further divided into three sub-types:

- **(i) Primary or Extractive Industry:** Involves extraction of natural resources directly from the earth, sea, or air. *Examples:* Agriculture, Mining, Fishing, Forestry, Dairy Farming.
- **(ii) Secondary or Manufacturing/Constructive Industry:** Converts raw materials into finished products through a manufacturing process. *Examples:* Iron and Steel Industry, Textile Industry, Cement Industry, Bakery, Sugar Mills.
- **(iii) Tertiary or Genetic/Service Industry:** Provides intangible services or is concerned with the breeding and multiplication of living organisms. *Examples:* Banking, Insurance, IT Services, Poultry Farming, Cattle Breeding.

**2. Commerce (Distribution and Exchange):**
Commerce includes all activities that facilitate the movement of goods from producers to consumers. It comprises:

- **(i) Trade:** Buying and selling of goods with the motive of earning profit. Trade is of two types:
  - *Internal Trade (Home Trade):* Trade within the geographical boundaries of a country. Sub-divided into Wholesale Trade and Retail Trade.
  - *International Trade (Foreign Trade):* Trade between two or more countries. Sub-divided into Import Trade, Export Trade, and Entrepôt Trade.
- **(ii) Auxiliaries to Trade:** Supporting services without which trade cannot function smoothly. These include:
  - *Transport* (movement of goods)
  - *Banking & Finance* (capital provision)
  - *Insurance* (risk coverage)
  - *Warehousing* (storage)
  - *Advertising* (promotion and sales)
  - *Communication* (information flow)
  - *Packaging* (protection and branding)

#### **Part (b) — Internal Trade vs International Trade (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (Tabular):**

| Basis of Difference | Internal Trade | International Trade |
|---|---|---|
| **Geographical Area** | Conducted within the country | Conducted between two or more countries |
| **Currency Used** | Single currency (INR) | Multiple currencies; foreign exchange involved |
| **Rules & Regulations** | Governed by domestic laws (Sale of Goods Act, GST) | Governed by international law, customs duties, FEMA, EXIM policy |
| **Risk Involved** | Comparatively lower | Higher — political, economic, currency, and tariff risks |
| **Documentation** | Simple (invoice, delivery challan) | Complex (Letter of Credit, Bill of Lading, Customs clearance) |
| **Settlement** | Cash, cheque, UPI, NEFT | Foreign remittances, forex transfers |
| **Examples** | A shop selling rice in Kerala | India exporting tea to UK; Kerala spices to UAE |
| **Government Role** | Limited | Heavy (DGFT, Customs, RBI for forex) |

**Conclusion:** While both forms of trade aim at the transfer of goods for profit, international trade adds layers of complexity due to cross-border regulations and currency conversion, making it a more specialized activity.

---

### **Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)**

**Q: What are the objectives of Business? Explain the modern concept of business with suitable examples.**
`[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]`

#### **Part (a) — Objectives of Business (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The objectives of business can be classified into **four broad categories**:

**1. Economic Objectives:**
These are the primary objectives aimed at ensuring the financial health of the business.
- **(i) Profit Earning:** To earn a reasonable return on capital invested.
- **(ii) Growth and Expansion:** To increase the size, market share, and turnover of the business.
- **(iii) Survival:** To remain competitive and continue operations.
- **(iv) Solvency:** To maintain adequate liquidity and a healthy balance sheet.

**2. Social Objectives:**
Businesses operate within a society and must serve it.
- Supplying quality goods at fair prices.
- Providing fair wages and good working conditions.
- Contributing to community welfare (CSR activities).
- Respecting environmental sustainability.

**3. Human / Personal Objectives:**
- Employee satisfaction and welfare.
- Skill development and training opportunities.
- Harmonious employer-employee relations.
- Work-life balance.

**4. National Objectives:**
- Generating employment.
- Promoting exports and reducing import dependency.
- Developing industrial self-reliance (Atmanirbhar Bharat).
- Contributing to GDP and tax revenues.

#### **Part (b) — Modern Concept of Business (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Traditional Concept:** Historically (especially in the era of Adam Smith and classical economists), business was viewed as a **purely economic activity** whose sole purpose was to **maximize profit** for the owner. Social welfare was considered outside the scope of business responsibility.

**Modern Concept:** In the contemporary view (popularized by management thinkers like **Peter Drucker**), business is seen as an **integral part of the social system**. According to this view:
> *"The purpose of business is not just to make a profit, but to create and keep a customer — profit follows naturally when customer satisfaction is achieved."*

**Key Features of the Modern Concept:**
1. **Customer-Centric Approach:** The customer is central; profit is a by-product of value creation.
2. **Social Responsibility:** Business must act in the interest of society (e.g., CSR under Companies Act 2013, Section 135).
3. **Triple Bottom Line (TBL):** Businesses must balance **People, Planet, and Profit**.
4. **Ethical Conduct:** Fair trade practices, transparency, and corporate governance.
5. **Stakeholder Approach:** Business serves all stakeholders — customers, employees, investors, government, community — not just shareholders.

**Suitable Examples:**
- **Tata Group:** Runs the Tata Trusts, contributes ~66% of profits to charity, exemplifying social commitment.
- **Infosys Foundation:** Invests in rural education, healthcare, and environmental conservation.
- **ITC Limited:** Has achieved carbon-positive, water-positive, and zero-waste status, demonstrating environmental stewardship.
- **Kerala Startup Mission (KSUM):** Government initiative to support eco-system businesses that combine profit with social impact.

**Conclusion:** The modern concept recognizes that sustainable profitability is inseparable from ethical behavior, customer satisfaction, and social welfare.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Conflating Industry & Commerce** — Students often interchange them. *Industry creates the product; Commerce moves it.* Losing 1–2 marks per occurrence.
> 2. **Forgetting Genetic Industry** — Always remember the *three* types: Primary, Secondary, **Tertiary (Genetic/Service)**. Missing this is a 1-mark loss.
> 3. **Writing only "profit" for business objectives** — Examiners expect a *classification* into economic, social, human, and national. A one-line answer gets 1–2 marks only.
> 4. **Omitting examples in long answers** — KTU values **illustrations** heavily; an answer without examples typically loses 2–3 marks out of 14.
> 5. **Spelling "Buisness" or "Comerce"** — Spelling errors in board exams cost marks.
> 6. **Treating Internal and International Trade as same** — Must highlight **currency, regulation, risk** differences explicitly.

---

## 🔹 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🎯

- ✅ **Business = Continuous + Economic + Exchange + Profit motive.** (Master equation.)
- ✅ **Three features every business MUST have:** Economic activity, Profit motive, Continuity.
- ✅ **Two major classifications:** **Industry** (production) and **Commerce** (distribution & exchange).
- ✅ **Three types of Industry:** Primary, Secondary, Tertiary (Genetic).
- ✅ **Commerce has two parts:** Trade + Auxiliaries to Trade.
- ✅ **Trade has two parts:** Internal (Wholesale + Retail) and International (Import + Export + Entrepôt).
- ✅ **Auxiliaries to Trade = 7 helpers:** Transport, Banking, Insurance, Warehousing, Advertising, Communication, Packaging.
- ✅ **Four types of Utility:** Form, Place, Time, Possession.
- ✅ **Modern Concept > Traditional Concept** — Profit is a *means*, Customer Satisfaction is the *goal*.
- ✅ **Triple Bottom Line:** People + Planet + Profit.
- ✅ **Peter Drucker** = Father of Modern Management thought.
- ✅ **Government Department ≠ Business** (no profit motive); **LIC, SBI = Business** (profit motive exists).
- ✅ For KTU exam: Always write in **headings + bullets + tables** format — examiners scan quickly.
- ✅ In Part B answers: Provide **definition → classification → diagram/example → conclusion** structure to maximize marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET834 - BUSINESS ORGANIZATION AND DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Concept

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Business** is an economic activity involving the regular production, purchase, sale, or exchange of goods and services with the primary objective of earning profit and satisfying human wants through the organized efforts of individuals and institutions operating within a legal, social, and economic framework.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme OEMET834 syllabus, the **Concept of Business** encompasses the foundational understanding of what constitutes a business activity, its distinguishing features, and its position within the broader economic system. The concept is built upon three pillars:

1. **Economic Activity** — involving production, distribution, and consumption of wealth.
2. **Continuous Process** — not a one-time transaction but a recurring engagement.
3. **Profit Motive** — the driving force, though modern businesses also recognize social objectives.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Business like a Beehive 🐝**
>
> Imagine a beehive. The bees don't randomly collect nectar from flowers — they systematically visit flowers (procure raw materials), process nectar into honey (production), store it in honeycombs (warehousing), and distribute it. The entire hive operates continuously, with each bee playing a specific role. The "honey" represents the goods/services produced, the "flowers" represent the market from which inputs are drawn, and the "queen bee" represents the entrepreneur who coordinates everything. Just as a hive cannot survive without producing honey, a business cannot sustain itself without delivering value consistently.
>
> **The Three Keys of a Business (like the Three Laws of the Hive):**
> - **Continuous Activity** → Bees work every day, not occasionally.
> - **Value Creation** → Raw nectar is transformed into honey.
> - **Purposeful Direction** → A queen bee ensures coordinated effort.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants in Business Concept

The following foundational metrics define the boundaries of any business activity:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Profit** is measured as **Revenue − Cost** and serves as the **primary performance indicator** of a business.
> - **Risk Bearing** is the **inverse function of certainty**; higher risk generally implies higher expected return.
> - **Utility Creation** is the **added value** transferred from producer to consumer — measured as the **difference between what the consumer is willing to pay and what they actually pay**.

### 1.4 KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the OEMET834 Module 1 syllabus framework, students must clearly distinguish between **Business, Profession, and Employment**, and understand that business is fundamentally distinct from charitable or religious activities despite sometimes overlapping in operational appearance.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Conceptual Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Breaking Down the Concept of Business — Structured Logic Steps

**Step 1: Identify the Economic Nature**
- Business is fundamentally an **economic activity**, meaning it is concerned with the creation, acquisition, sale, or transfer of economic goods and services.
- It is **NOT** a religious activity (which is spiritual), a charitable activity (which is welfare-oriented), or a social activity (which is relationship-oriented).
- The **economic character** is what distinguishes business from other forms of human activity.

**Step 2: Recognize the Continuity Element**
- A single transaction (e.g., selling your old car) is **NOT** a business.
- **Regularity** is the hallmark — transactions must occur **repeatedly and systematically**.
- This is why a part-time street vendor qualifies as a business if they operate regularly.

**Step 3: Acknowledge the Profit Motive**
- While **modern businesses** also pursue social objectives (CSR, sustainability), the **primary motivator** remains profit.
- **Profit** is the **reward for risk-bearing** and the **indicator of efficiency**.

**Step 4: Establish the Legal-Social Framework**
- Every business operates within a **legal framework** (Companies Act, GST laws, labour laws).
- It also operates within a **social framework** (cultural norms, ethical expectations).
- The **Concept of Business** therefore includes compliance and responsibility dimensions.

### 2.2 Why and How — The Deep Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Profit Essential?**
> Profit is not greed — it is the **lifeblood** that allows a business to:
> - Reinvest in technology and expansion
> - Reward shareholders and employees
> - Survive economic downturns
> - Serve society through taxes and CSR
>
> **How does Business Differ from Charity?**
> A charity receives donations to fund its mission. A business receives payment in exchange for value delivered. The **reciprocity** in a business transaction must create **mutual benefit**; in charity, the giver expects no direct return.

### 2.3 KTU Conceptual Cheat Sheet (Module 1)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates the **high-yield concepts** from Module 1 of OEMET834 for rapid revision. Each row captures a core concept, its distinguishing attribute, and its KTU exam relevance.

| Concept | Defining Attribute | KTU Exam Significance | Distinguishing Marker |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Business** | Economic activity with profit motive | Most frequently asked in Part A (3-mark Q) | Continuous + Profit + Legal |
| **Profession** | Service-based activity requiring specialized knowledge | Common in comparison-based questions | Code of Conduct + Degree required |
| **Employment** | Working under another's direction for wages | Often asked as a contrast question | Subordination + Fixed Wage |
| **Trade** | Buying and selling of goods | Sub-concept of business | Exchange focused |
| **Commerce** | Auxiliaries to trade | Sometimes confused with business | Includes transport, banking, insurance |
| **Industry** | Production of goods | Sub-concept of business | Manufacturing focused |
| **Profit** | Excess of revenue over cost | Quantitative concept | $\text{Profit} = \text{Revenue} - \text{Cost}$ |
| **Risk** | Uncertainty of outcome | Qualitative concept | Inverse of certainty |
| **Utility** | Want-satisfying power | Theoretical concept | Form, Place, Time, Possession |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of the Concept of Business

The understanding of business concepts is **foundational** for:

- **Entrepreneurship Development** — Identifying business opportunities and risks.
- **Corporate Strategy** — Deciding whether an entity is operating as a business, profession, or employment.
- **Economic Policy Making** — Governments classify activities as business for taxation purposes.
- **Legal Compliance** — Determining applicability of business laws (Companies Act, Contract Act).
- **Investment Decisions** — Investors evaluate whether an activity constitutes a viable business.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Classification Matrix

### 3.1 Conceptual Derivation — Defining Business Step-by-Step

Since this is a **conceptual management topic** (not a numerical engineering topic), the derivation here is **logical and definitional** rather than mathematical. The framework below shows how the concept of business is built up logically.

**Step 1: Begin with the Generic Definition**

$$B_{\text{generic}} = \{x \mid x \text{ is an economic activity}\}$$

**Step 2: Add the Continuity Constraint**

$$B_{\text{continuous}} = \{x \mid x \text{ is an economic activity AND } x \text{ is repeated regularly}\}$$

**Step 3: Add the Profit Motive**

$$B_{\text{profit}} = \{x \mid x \text{ is continuous AND } x \text{ aims to earn profit}\}$$

**Step 4: Add the Legal Framework**

$$B_{\text{final}} = \{x \mid x \text{ is continuous AND profit-seeking AND legally organized}\}$$

**Step 5: Convert to Plain Language**

> [!NOTE]
> **In Plain English:** Business is any legally organized, continuous economic activity carried out with the primary goal of earning profit by satisfying human wants.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Business vs. Profession vs. Employment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is the **single most frequently tested table** in OEMET834 Module 1. Students are expected to reproduce this comparison in both Part A (short) and Part B (long) questions.

| Basis of Difference | Business | Profession | Employment |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Meaning** | Economic activity for profit | Service requiring specialized knowledge | Working under an employer for wages |
| **Nature of Work** | Production, purchase, sale of goods/services | Specialized service (law, medicine, accounting) | Performance of assigned duties |
| **Qualification Required** | Not mandatory | Formal degree/license mandatory | Depends on job role |
| **Risk Bearing** | Owner bears all risk | Practitioner bears moderate risk | Employee bears no risk |
| **Reward** | Profit | Professional fees | Salary/Wages |
| **Code of Conduct** | Governed by business laws | Governed by professional body ethics | Governed by employment contract |
| **Transfer of Interest** | Can be transferred/sold | Cannot be transferred freely | Cannot be transferred |
| **Examples** | Reliance Industries, Apple Inc. | Chartered Accountant, Doctor, Lawyer | School Teacher, Bank Clerk |

### 3.3 Classification of Business Activities — Analytical Breakdown

Business activities are classified into **three primary categories**. Each category serves a distinct economic function:

**Category 1: Industry**
- Industry refers to activities concerned with **production of goods** or **rendering of services**.
- Sub-categories include:
  - **Primary Industry** — Agriculture, mining, fishing (raw material extraction).
  - **Secondary Industry** — Manufacturing, construction (transformation of raw materials).
  - **Tertiary Industry** — Banking, transport, insurance (service provision).

**Category 2: Commerce**
- Commerce includes **all activities that facilitate the exchange of goods**.
- It encompasses **trade** (buying and selling) and **auxiliaries to trade** (transport, banking, insurance, warehousing, advertising).

**Category 3: Trade**
- Trade refers specifically to the **buying and selling of goods**.
- Sub-types include:
  - **Internal Trade** — Within the same country (wholesale, retail).
  - **External Trade** — Between countries (import, export).

### 3.4 Mathematical Representation of Profit (Conceptual Formula)

While this module is primarily conceptual, a basic profit equation aids understanding:

$$\text{Profit} = \text{Total Revenue} - \text{Total Cost}$$

$$\text{Total Revenue} = \text{Price per Unit} \times \text{Quantity Sold}$$

$$\text{Total Cost} = \text{Fixed Cost} + \text{Variable Cost}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The profit equation formalizes the **economic outcome** of a business activity. A business is considered successful only if $\text{Profit} > 0$ over the long term. Short-term losses may be tolerated (e.g., during expansion), but sustained losses indicate that the activity may not qualify as a viable business.

### 3.5 Features/Characteristics of Business — Analytical Framework

The **KTU Board Exam** frequently asks students to "List and explain the features of business." The following 8 characteristics are mandatory for full marks:

1. **Economic Activity** — Involves production, distribution, or consumption of wealth.
2. **Profit Motive** — Primary objective is earning profits.
3. **Continuous Process** — Not a one-time transaction.
4. **Legal Compliance** — Operates within the framework of law.
5. **Risk and Uncertainty** — Outcomes are not guaranteed; risk is inherent.
6. **Customer Focus** — Success depends on satisfying customer needs.
7. **Employment Generation** — Creates jobs and economic opportunities.
8. **Social Responsibility** — Modern businesses also serve social goals (CSR).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid node IDs below follow the **Alpha Rule** (prefixed with letters, purely alphanumeric). No reserved keywords are used as standalone node names. All labels are **plain uppercase alphanumeric** with no markdown formatting inside double quotes.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1:** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**"Define Business and list any four features of business."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Business may be defined as an economic activity involving the regular production, purchase, sale, or exchange of goods and services, carried out with the primary objective of earning profit and satisfying human wants.

**Four Features (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Economic Activity** — Business is concerned with the creation, acquisition, and distribution of wealth; it is not a religious or charitable activity.

2. **Profit Motive** — The primary objective of every business is to earn profit, which serves as a measure of efficiency and a reward for risk-bearing.

3. **Continuous Process** — Business is not a one-time transaction but a regular and recurring engagement in production or exchange activities.

4. **Risk and Uncertainty** — Every business operates in an environment of uncertainty; outcomes are influenced by market dynamics, consumer behaviour, and external shocks.

---

**Question 2:** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**"Distinguish between Business and Profession."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1.5 Marks each side):**

| Basis | Business | Profession |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Nature** | Economic activity involving production and sale | Specialized service based on expertise |
| **Reward** | Profit from the venture | Professional fees for services |
| **Risk Bearing** | Owner bears all business risk | Risk is moderate and limited |
| **Qualification** | No formal qualification mandatory | Formal degree and license mandatory |
| **Code of Conduct** | Governed by business laws | Governed by professional body ethics |
| **Example** | A textile manufacturing company | A Chartered Accountant firm |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1 Choice Q1]**
**"Explain in detail the concept of business. Discuss its nature, scope, and significance in modern economic development."**

**Model Answer Structure:**

#### Part (a) — Concept and Nature of Business (7 Marks)

**1. Definition (2 Marks):**
Business is a legally organized economic activity carried out continuously with the primary objective of earning profit by producing, buying, selling, or exchanging goods and services to satisfy human wants.

**2. Nature of Business (3 Marks):**
- It is an **economic activity** as it involves production and distribution of wealth.
- It is a **continuous process** and not a single isolated transaction.
- It is carried out with the **profit motive** as the primary objective.
- It operates within a **legal and social framework**.
- It involves **risk-bearing** and **uncertainty of outcomes**.

**3. Features of Business (2 Marks):**
Eight core features include economic nature, profit motive, continuity, risk-bearing, legal compliance, customer focus, employment generation, and social responsibility.

#### Part (b) — Scope and Significance in Modern Economic Development (7 Marks)

**1. Scope of Business (3 Marks):**
The scope of business extends across three dimensions:
- **Industry** — Primary, secondary, and tertiary industries covering extraction, manufacturing, and services.
- **Commerce** — Trade and auxiliaries including banking, transport, insurance, and warehousing.
- **Trade** — Internal trade (wholesale and retail) and external trade (imports and exports).

**2. Significance in Economic Development (4 Marks):**
- **Capital Formation** — Businesses generate savings and channel them into productive investments.
- **Employment Generation** — Creates jobs across sectors, reducing unemployment.
- **Infrastructure Development** — Drives demand for roads, ports, power, and digital infrastructure.
- **Innovation and Technology** — Businesses invest in R&D, leading to technological progress.
- **Standard of Living** — By producing a variety of goods and services, businesses enhance quality of life.
- **Global Integration** — Through external trade, businesses connect the domestic economy to global markets.
- **Government Revenue** — Businesses pay taxes that fund public services and welfare programs.

---

### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1 Choice Q2]**
**"Discuss the classification of business activities. Compare and contrast Business, Profession, and Employment with suitable examples."**

**Model Answer Structure:**

#### Part (a) — Classification of Business Activities (7 Marks)

**1. Industry (2.5 Marks):**
Industry refers to activities involved in the production of goods and rendering of services. It is classified into:
- **Primary Industry** — Concerned with extraction of natural resources (e.g., agriculture, mining, fishing).
- **Secondary Industry** — Concerned with converting raw materials into finished goods (e.g., manufacturing, construction).
- **Tertiary Industry** — Concerned with providing services (e.g., banking, transportation, healthcare, IT services).

**2. Commerce (2.5 Marks):**
Commerce includes all activities that facilitate the buying and selling of goods. It comprises trade and auxiliaries to trade such as transport, banking, insurance, warehousing, and advertising.

**3. Trade (2 Marks):**
Trade refers specifically to the buying and selling of goods and is classified into:
- **Internal Trade** — Trade within the country (wholesale trade and retail trade).
- **External Trade** — Trade between countries (import trade and export trade).

#### Part (b) — Comparison of Business, Profession, and Employment (7 Marks)

| Basis | Business | Profession | Employment |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Definition** | Economic activity for profit | Service requiring specialized knowledge | Working under employer for wages |
| **Risk** | Owner bears all risk | Moderate risk | No risk for employee |
| **Reward** | Profit | Professional fees | Salary/Wages |
| **Qualification** | Not mandatory | Mandatory degree/license | Depends on job |
| **Capital** | Significant investment required | Minimal capital needed | No capital required |
| **Examples** | TATA Motors, Infosys | Doctor, Lawyer, CA | Teacher, Clerk, Engineer |
| **Transferability** | Business can be sold/transferred | Cannot be easily transferred | Job cannot be transferred |

**Conclusion (1 Mark):**
While business, profession, and employment are all means of livelihood, they differ fundamentally in terms of risk-bearing, reward structure, qualification requirements, and the nature of work performed. Understanding these distinctions is essential for career planning and economic decision-making.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on "Concept of Business" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Business with Trade** — Business is a **broader concept** that includes industry, commerce, and trade. Students who define business merely as "buying and selling" lose 2–3 marks immediately.
>
> 2. **Omitting Continuity** — Many students forget to mention that business is a **continuous process**. A one-time sale is not a business. Examiners specifically look for this keyword.
>
> 3. **Ignoring Modern Objectives** — In 2024, the KTU syllabus emphasizes that businesses have **dual objectives** — economic AND social. Students who only mention profit lose marks.
>
> 4. **Weak Comparative Tables** — When comparing Business vs Profession vs Employment, students often present a **one-sided answer** instead of a **balanced comparison table**. Always use a table format for full marks.
>
> 5. **No Examples** — The KTU 2024 valuation key explicitly awards **0.5 to 1 mark for examples**. Students who fail to cite examples (e.g., Reliance, TATA, a local shop) lose easy marks.
>
> 6. **Conflating Risk with Loss** — Risk does **NOT** mean certain loss. It means **uncertainty of outcome**. This distinction is a favourite KTU trap question.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Concept of Business (Module 1, OEMET834)**

- ✅ **Business** = Economic Activity + Continuous Process + Profit Motive + Legal Framework + Risk Bearing.

- ✅ A **single transaction** is **NOT** business; **regularity** is essential.

- ✅ **Profit** = Total Revenue − Total Cost; it is the **primary motivator** but **not the only objective** in modern business.

- ✅ Business activities are classified into **Industry, Commerce, and Trade**.

- ✅ **Industry** has three sub-types: **Primary, Secondary, and Tertiary**.

- ✅ **Commerce** includes **Trade + Auxiliaries to Trade** (transport, banking, insurance, warehousing, advertising).

- ✅ **Trade** is classified into **Internal Trade** (wholesale + retail) and **External Trade** (import + export).

- ✅ **Business vs Profession vs Employment** — Differ on the basis of **risk, reward, qualification, capital, and transferability**.

- ✅ **Modern business objectives** include both **economic objectives** (profit, growth) and **social objectives** (CSR, environment, welfare).

- ✅ **Risk and Uncertainty** are **inherent** to business — they cannot be eliminated, only managed.

- ✅ **Legal compliance** is non-negotiable — every business must operate within the framework of laws like the Companies Act, GST Act, and Labour Laws.

- ✅ The **Concept of Business** is the **foundation** upon which all subsequent modules (organization, management, finance) are built — master this first.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Objectives
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET834 - BUSINESS ORGANIZATION AND DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Objectives

# Objectives of Business — A Foundational Study

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Business Objectives** are the predetermined, measurable ends towards which a business enterprise directs its resources, activities, and efforts. According to classical business theory, every business operates with a **hierarchy of objectives** — starting from the fundamental purpose of existence (mission) down to specific, time-bound targets (goals).

In simple terms, *objectives* answer the question: **"What is the business trying to achieve?"**

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a business like a **ship sailing across an ocean**:

- The **destination** (say, a port city) represents the **mission** — the broad purpose.
- The **coordinates** entered into the GPS represent the **objectives** — the specific, measurable checkpoints.
- The **daily distance covered** represents the **goals** — short-term targets that move the ship toward the destination.

Without objectives, the ship would simply drift — using fuel but reaching nowhere. Similarly, a business without objectives spends resources without direction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 introduces students to the **foundations of business**, of which *Objectives* is a core sub-topic. Students must be able to:
> - Differentiate between **aims, objectives, and goals**
> - Explain the **classification of business objectives** (Economic, Human, Social, National)
> - Understand the **hierarchy and interrelationship** of objectives in a modern enterprise

### Key Terms at a Glance

| Term | Meaning | Example |
|------|---------|---------|
| **Mission** | Fundamental purpose of existence | "To provide affordable mobility" |
| **Objective** | Specific end to be achieved | "Achieve 15% market share in Kerala by 2026" |
| **Goal** | Short-term, quantified target | "Sell 5,000 units this quarter" |
| **Aim** | General direction or aspiration | "Become a market leader" |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## The Logic Behind Business Objectives

### Step-by-Step Reasoning

- **Why do businesses need objectives?**
  Objectives provide **direction**, **motivation**, **coordination**, and a **standard for evaluation**. Without them, decision-making becomes random and resources are wasted.

- **How are objectives formulated?**
  They flow from the **mission** of the organization, are influenced by **stakeholders** (owners, employees, customers, government, society), and must be **realistic, measurable, and time-bound**.

- **What characteristics must objectives possess?**
  Objectives must be **Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, and Time-bound** (the SMART framework widely tested in KTU examinations).

### Classification of Business Objectives (The 4-Domain Model)

Business objectives fall into **four broad categories**, each answering a different stakeholder question:

1. **Economic Objectives** — *What does the business owe to itself?*
   - Profit maximization (classical view)
   - Sales maximization (Baumol's theory)
   - Growth and expansion
   - Survival and continuity
   - Market leadership

2. **Human Objectives** — *What does the business owe to its people?*
   - Fair wages and salaries
   - Employee welfare and well-being
   - Job satisfaction and security
   - Training and development opportunities
   - Healthy working conditions

3. **Social Objectives** — *What does the business owe to society?*
   - Consumer protection and fair pricing
   - Environmental protection and sustainability
   - Ethical business practices
   - Community development
   - Philanthropy and CSR (Corporate Social Responsibility)

4. **National Objectives** — *What does the business owe to the nation?*
   - Contributing to GDP and economic growth
   - Generating employment
   - Supporting government policies (Make in India, Digital India)
   - Export promotion and import substitution
   - Adhering to national priorities

### The "Why" Behind Each Category — Quick Logic Grid

| Category | Core Question | Primary Stakeholder | KTU-Relevant Keyword |
|----------|---------------|---------------------|----------------------|
| Economic | Profit or growth? | Owners / Shareholders | **Profit Maximization** |
| Human | Employee welfare? | Employees / Workers | **Industrial Harmony** |
| Social | Public good? | Community / Consumers | **CSR & Ethics** |
| National | Country's interest? | Government / Nation | **Economic Development** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> Classical economists (e.g., **Adam Smith**) emphasized *profit maximization* as the sole objective.
> Modern management thinkers (e.g., **Peter Drucker**) argue for *multiple objectives* — profit being one among many. This shift is a frequently asked question in KTU Module 1.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Key Point | Exam Tip |
|---------|-----------|----------|
| Mission vs Objective | Mission = "What" ; Objective = "How much / When" | Use a real company example (e.g., Tata, Infosys) |
| SMART Objectives | Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound | Often asked as a **3-mark short note** |
| Classical vs Modern Objectives | Classical = Profit only ; Modern = Multiple objectives | Most common **14-mark question** in Module 1 |
| Stakeholders | Owners, employees, customers, suppliers, government, society | Draw a stakeholder diagram for full marks |

### Real-World Utility

In engineering and technology businesses (which is the eventual domain of KTU B.Tech students), objectives translate into:
- **R&D targets** (e.g., "develop a low-cost EV by 2027")
- **Quality benchmarks** (e.g., "achieve Six Sigma by FY2026")
- **Sustainability goals** (e.g., "carbon-neutral operations by 2030")

Understanding objectives helps future engineers **align technical decisions with business goals** — a critical skill in industry 4.0 workplaces.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

Since this is a **conceptual management topic**, the "derivation" here refers to the **logical derivation of the objective hierarchy** and the **analytical framework** connecting mission to goals.

## Analytical Derivation: From Mission to Goals

### Step 1: Define the Mission (Broadest Level)

The mission is the **fundamental reason** the business exists.

$$
\text{Mission} = \text{Purpose of Existence} + \text{Core Values} + \text{Stakeholder Promise}
$$

**Example:** Infosys's mission is *"to achieve our objectives in an environment of fairness, honesty, and courtesy towards our clients, employees, vendors, and society at large."*

### Step 2: Translate Mission into Strategic Objectives

Objectives are **specific, measurable outcomes** that operationalize the mission.

$$
\text{Strategic Objective} = f(\text{Mission}, \text{Environment}, \text{Capabilities})
$$

**Example:** A strategic objective might be *"To achieve \$10 billion in revenue by FY2030."*

### Step 3: Cascade Objectives into Tactical Goals

Goals are **short-term, department-specific** targets.

$$
\text{Goal} = \text{Objective} \times \text{Time Horizon} \times \text{Department}
$$

**Example:** *"The marketing department will generate 2,000 qualified leads per month this quarter."*

### Step 4: Verify with the SMART Test

Each objective must pass the **SMART criteria**:

$$
\text{Valid Objective} = (\text{Specific}) \land (\text{Measurable}) \land (\text{Achievable}) \land (\text{Relevant}) \land (\text{Time-bound})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** When writing 14-mark answers, always present objectives in a **pyramid format** — Mission at the top, Objectives in the middle, Goals at the bottom. This visual hierarchy earns extra presentation marks.

## Analytical Matrix: Classical vs Modern View of Business Objectives

| Dimension | Classical View (Profit Maximization) | Modern View (Multiple Objectives) |
|-----------|--------------------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| **Proponent** | Adam Smith, Milton Friedman | Peter Drucker, modern management schools |
| **Primary Goal** | Single — maximize profit | Multiple — profit, growth, welfare, CSR |
| **Stakeholder Focus** | Shareholders only | All stakeholders |
| **Time Horizon** | Short-term profit | Long-term sustainability |
| **Social Responsibility** | Minimal / optional | Integral to business |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | High (foundational theory) | Very High (contemporary application) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:**
> A common 14-mark question is: *"Discuss the various objectives of business. Are economic objectives sufficient in the modern context?"*
> The model answer should:
> 1. Define objectives (3 marks)
> 2. List and explain the 4 categories (6 marks)
> 3. Argue why modern businesses need multiple objectives (3 marks)
> 4. Conclude with a balanced view (2 marks)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: The Hierarchy of Business Objectives (Pyramid Model)

This Mermaid diagram illustrates the **cascading relationship** from broad mission down to specific, measurable goals.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    mission["MISSION - Fundamental Purpose of Existence"]:::top
    vision["VISION - Future Aspiration and Direction"]:::level1
    objEcon["ECONOMIC OBJECTIVES - Profit, Growth, Survival"]:::level2
    objHuman["HUMAN OBJECTIVES - Welfare, Training, Satisfaction"]:::level2
    objSocial["SOCIAL OBJECTIVES - CSR, Ethics, Environment"]:::level2
    objNatl["NATIONAL OBJECTIVES - GDP Contribution, Employment"]:::level2
    goal1["SHORT TERM GOAL - Quarterly Sales Target"]:::base
    goal2["SHORT TERM GOAL - Monthly Production Output"]:::base
    goal3["SHORT TERM GOAL - Weekly Quality Checks"]:::base

    mission --> vision
    vision --> objEcon
    vision --> objHuman
    vision --> objSocial
    vision --> objNatl
    objEcon --> goal1
    objHuman --> goal2
    objSocial --> goal3

    classDef top fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef level1 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef level2 fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef base fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

## Diagram 2: Stakeholder-Objective Mapping (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

Since this topic involves multiple stakeholders and competing objectives, the following **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** maps how each stakeholder influences and is influenced by business objectives.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    owner["OWNERS / SHAREHOLDERS"]:::a
    employee["EMPLOYEES / WORKERS"]:::b
    customer["CUSTOMERS / CONSUMERS"]:::c
    supplier["SUPPLIERS / VENDORS"]:::d
    govt["GOVERNMENT / REGULATORS"]:::e
    society["SOCIETY / COMMUNITY"]:::f

    core["CENTRAL BUSINESS ENTERPRISE - The Organization"]:::core

    ownerReq["Economic Returns and Growth"]:::r
    empReq["Wages, Welfare, Job Security"]:::r
    custReq["Quality Products, Fair Price"]:::r
    suppReq["Timely Payments, Long-term Contracts"]:::r
    govtReq["Taxes, Compliance, Employment"]:::r
    socReq["Ethical Behavior, Environmental Care"]:::r

    owner --> core
    employee --> core
    customer --> core
    supplier --> core
    govt --> core
    society --> core

    core --> ownerReq
    core --> empReq
    core --> custReq
    core --> suppReq
    core --> govtReq
    core --> socReq

    ownerReq -.satisfies.-> owner
    empReq -.satisfies.-> employee
    custReq -.satisfies.-> customer
    suppReq -.satisfies.-> supplier
    govtReq -.satisfies.-> govt
    socReq -.satisfies.-> society

    classDef a fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef b fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef c fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef d fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef e fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef f fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```
