## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 KTU Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
*Define the term "Industrial Policy Resolution, 1956." What was its significance in shaping the Indian business scenario?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Industrial Policy Resolution (IPR), 1956** was the second post-independence industrial policy framework formulated by the Government of India. It was based on the **Mahalanobis Model** of economic development and officially adopted the **"Socialist Pattern of Society"** as the national economic objective.

**Significance (3 points — 1 mark each):**

1. **Public Sector Expansion:** It reserved **17 key industries** exclusively for the public sector, including defense, atomic energy, iron and steel, coal, heavy machinery, and telecommunications. This laid the foundation for the **Navratna and Maharatna** PSU system.
2. **Industrial Licensing Regime:** It established a comprehensive licensing system under the **Industries (Development & Regulation) Act, 1951**, giving the state tight control over private industrial investment.
3. **Foundation for Five-Year Plans:** It provided the structural framework for India's **command-economy** model until the 1991 LPG reforms.

---

**Question 2.** [KTU University Exam — December 2022]
*What is meant by "Liberalization-Privatization-Globalization (LPG) Reforms" of 1991? Briefly explain any two triggers for these reforms.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **LPG Reforms of 1991** refer to the comprehensive economic policy package introduced by the Government of India under **Prime Minister P.V. Narasimha Rao** and **Finance Minister Dr. Manmohan Singh** to transition India from a **centrally-planned socialist economy** to a **market-oriented open economy**.

**Two Triggers (2 marks):**

1. **Balance of Payments (BoP) Crisis, 1991:** India's foreign exchange reserves had fallen to a level sufficient for only **two weeks of essential imports**. The country was forced to pledge its gold reserves to the **International Monetary Fund (IMF)** as collateral for emergency loans.
2. **Collapse of the Soviet Union (1991):** The end of the Cold War and the disintegration of the USSR, India's primary Cold War ally, exposed the vulnerabilities of the **command economy** model and eliminated the ideological cushion supporting the pre-1991 policy stance.

(Alternative valid trigger: **Inflationary pressure**, **falling industrial productivity**, and **gulf war oil price shock of 1990**.)

---

### 5.2 KTU Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Question A.** [KTU University Exam — December 2023]
*(a)* Explain in detail the evolution of Indian Industrial Policy from 1947 to 1991, highlighting the key features of each policy. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Discuss the salient features of the 1991 LPG Reforms. What impact have these reforms had on the Indian business scenario? *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Evolution of Indian Industrial Policy (1947–1991):**

**1. Industrial Policy Resolution, 1948 (2 marks):**
- Framed under the leadership of **Jawaharlal Nehru**.
- Adopted the **Mixed Economy** principle.
- Categorized industries into:
  - **Strategic industries** (4 industries) — state monopoly: defense, atomic energy, railways, aircraft.
  - **Basic/key industries** (6 industries) — state dominance: coal, iron & steel, shipbuilding, etc.
  - **Remaining industries** — open to private enterprise.
- Set the foundation for state intervention in industrial development.

**2. Industrial Policy Resolution, 1956 (3 marks):**
- Adopted the **"Socialist Pattern of Society"** as the national objective.
- Recommended by the **Sarkar Committee**.
- **17 industries reserved for the public sector**.
- Expanded the licensing regime under the **IDR Act, 1951**.
- Emphasized **heavy industry** as the engine of growth (Mahalanobis Model).
- Remained the dominant policy framework for 35 years.

**3. Industrial Policy Statement, 1977 (1 mark):**
- Framed by the **Janata Party** government.
- Shifted focus to **rural industrialization**, small-scale sector, and labor-intensive industries.
- Promoted the **Antyodaya** philosophy.

**4. Industrial Policy Statement, 1980 (1 mark):**
- Framed by **Indira Gandhi's** Congress government.
- Restored the importance of the public sector.
- Introduced the **"commanding heights"** concept for PSUs.
- Began export promotion measures.

**Common Thread:** All pre-1991 policies favored state control, public sector dominance, and tight licensing — the cumulative result was the **1991 BoP crisis**.

---

**(b) 1991 LPG Reforms — Salient Features and Impact:**

**Salient Features (4 marks):**

**Liberalization (1.5 marks):**
- Abolition of **industrial licensing** for all but 5 strategic industries.
- Removal of **price controls** on most products.
- Reduction in **import tariffs** — peak duty from 300%+ to 10%.
- **FEMA, 1999** (replacing FERA 1973) — liberalized foreign exchange.
- **SEBI Act, 1992** — capital market regulator established.

**Privatization (1.5 marks):**
- **Disinvestment Commission** established in 1996.
- Reduction of public sector's role from operator to **regulator**.
- PSUs reclassified as **Navratna, Miniratna, Maharatna** based on performance.
- Disinvestment in VSNL, BALCO, Maruti Udyog.

**Globalization (1 mark):**
- **100% FDI** permitted in many sectors under the automatic route.
- India became a **founding member of the WTO (1995)**.
- **TRIPS, TRIMS, GATS** obligations accepted.
- **Foreign Trade Policy** liberalized; **SEZ Act 2005** enacted.

**Impact (3 marks):**

| Impact Area | Before 1991 | After 1991 |
|---|---|---|
| GDP Growth Rate | Hindu rate of growth (~3.5%) | 6–7% sustained |
| FDI Inflows | Negligible | $85+ billion annually |
| Public Sector | Dominant | Strategic and regulated |
| Consumer Choice | Limited | Diverse and competitive |
| Technology Access | Restricted | Global-standard technology available |
| Entrepreneurship | Stifled | Startup ecosystem emerged |

**Conclusion:** The 1991 reforms transformed India from a closed, state-controlled economy into one of the world's most dynamic emerging markets.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**Question B.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
*(a)* Discuss the structure of the Indian industrial sector — public, private, joint, and cooperative. Highlight the role and challenges of the **public sector** in the contemporary Indian business scenario. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* What is **FDI**? Explain the **automatic and government routes** for FDI in India. What is the significance of the **Make in India** initiative in attracting foreign investment? *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Structure of Indian Industrial Sector and Role of Public Sector:**

**1. Public Sector (3 marks):**
- **Definition:** Enterprises owned, controlled, and managed by the government (Central or State).
- **Examples:** BHEL, SAIL, ONGC, NTPC, Indian Railways, BSNL, LIC, Air India.
- **Classification:** Maharatna (10 PSUs), Navratna (14 PSUs), Miniratna.
- **Roles:** Strategic sectors (defense, atomic energy), natural monopolies (railways), social goods (public health infrastructure), and economic stabilization.
- **Contemporary Challenges:**
  - Inefficiency and bureaucratic delays.
  - Political interference in operational decisions.
  - Heavy reliance on government subsidies — fiscal burden.
  - Inability to compete with private sector agility in a globalized market.
  - Disinvestment delays due to political and labor union opposition.

**2. Private Sector (1.5 marks):**
- **Domestic private sector** includes large Indian business houses — Tata Group, Reliance Industries, Adani Group, Birla Group, Mahindra & Mahindra, Godrej.
- Pre-1991: tightly regulated by industrial licensing.
- Post-1991: emerged as the **engine of growth** in services, manufacturing, and startups.

**3. Joint Sector (1 mark):**
- **Public-Private Partnership (PPP)** model — co-ownership of public and private entities.
- Examples: Maruti Suzuki (originally), Delhi Metro Rail Corporation, Bangalore International Airport.
- Combines **public accountability** with **private efficiency**.

**4. Cooperative Sector (1.5 marks):**
- Member-owned, member-controlled, and member-benefited enterprises.
- Operate on **cooperative principles** (Rochdale Principles).
- Iconic example: **Amul (GCMMF)** — India's largest dairy cooperative, founded by **Dr. Verghese Kurien**.
- Also: Indian Farmers Fertiliser Cooperative (IFFCO), Krishak Bharati Cooperative Limited (KRIBHCO).
- Promotes **inclusive growth** and **rural development**.

---

**(b) FDI Routes and Make in India Significance:**

**1. Definition of FDI (1 mark):**
**Foreign Direct Investment (FDI)** is an investment made by a foreign entity (company, individual, government) in the business interests of another country, with the intent of establishing a lasting interest, management control, and long-term relationship (typically with 10% or more voting stock).

**2. FDI Routes in India (3 marks):**

| Route | Approval Required | Approval Body | Sectors |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Automatic Route** | No prior government approval | No approval needed | Most sectors including telecom, IT, e-commerce, single-brand retail |
| **Government Route** | Prior government approval required | Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT) | Strategic sectors: defence (above 74%), multi-brand retail, banking above 74% |

**Key Sectoral FDI Caps (2 marks):**
- **Defence:** Up to 74% automatic, above 74% with conditions.
- **Insurance:** 74% automatic.
- **Telecom:** 100% automatic.
- **E-commerce (B2B & marketplace):** 100% automatic.
- **Multi-brand retail:** 51% via government route.

**3. Make in India Initiative — Significance (3 marks):**

The **Make in India** initiative was launched by **Prime Minister Narendra Modi on September 25, 2014**, with the objective of boosting domestic manufacturing and attracting foreign investment.

**Significance:**

1. **Manufacturing GDP:** Targeted to increase the share of manufacturing in GDP from ~16% to **25% by 2025** (Amrit Kaal target).
2. **FDI Multiplier Effect:** FDI inflows grew from ~$36 billion in 2013-14 to over $85 billion in 2021-22 — a 2.4x increase.
3. **Employment Generation:** Sectors like mobile phone manufacturing, electronics, defence, and medical devices created **millions of jobs**.
4. **Technology Transfer:** Enabled Indian companies to access global best practices.
5. **PLI Synergy:** The 2020 **Production Linked Incentive (PLI) scheme** complements Make in India with ₹1.97 lakh crore outlay across 14 sectors.
6. **Reduction of Import Dependence:** Particularly in strategic sectors like electronics and pharmaceuticals.

**Real-world example:** Apple began manufacturing iPhones in **Foxconn's Tamil Nadu facility** in 2017, scaling to assemble iPhone 15 in India by 2023 — a direct outcome of Make in India.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Year Mismatches (–1 mark):** Confusing 1956 (IPR) with 1948 (IPR), or attributing the LPG reforms to Rajiv Gandhi (it was **Narasimha Rao + Manmohan Singh in 1991**).
> 2. **Incomplete LPG Coverage (–2 marks):** Writing only "globalization" without explicit explanation of **Liberalization** (industrial licensing abolition) and **Privatization** (disinvestment). Always use the **L-P-G triad** in order.
> 3. **No Causal Link to BoP Crisis (–1 mark):** Forgetting to mention the **1991 Balance of Payments crisis** as the trigger for reforms — KTU examiners reward causal thinking.
> 4. **MSME Classification Errors (–2 marks):** Using the OLD classification (investment in plant & machinery only) instead of the **2020 composite criteria** (investment AND turnover).
> 5. **No Examples in Long Essays (–2 marks):** Failing to cite real-world examples (Amul, Tata, VSNL, Foxconn-Apple) is the single most common reason for partial credit in Part B.
> 6. **Forgetting Modern Reforms (–1 mark):** Omitting **GST 2017, IBC 2016, Make in India 2014, Startup India 2016, PLI 2020** reduces the contemporary relevance of your answer.
> 7. **Diagram Negligence (–1 to –2 marks):** Not drawing a flow diagram for industrial policy evolution. The KTU 2024 OBE framework specifically rewards **visual learning outputs** — always include a self-drawn diagram for Part B answers.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

**Rapid-Revision Checklist for the Indian Scenario:**

- The **IPR 1948** introduced the **Mixed Economy**; the **IPR 1956** reserved **17 industries** for the public sector under the **Socialist Pattern**.
- The **1991 BoP crisis** (forex reserves for 2 weeks) triggered the **LPG Reforms** under **Narasimha Rao** and **Dr. Manmohan Singh**.
- **L = Liberalization** (licensing abolished), **P = Privatization** (disinvestment of PSUs), **G = Globalization** (FDI + WTO 1995).
- **FEMA 1999** replaced **FERA 1973**; **SEBI 1992** regulates capital markets.
- **MSME Act 2006** (revised 2020) — composite criteria based on **investment AND turnover** (Micro: ₹1 cr / ₹5 cr; Small: ₹10 cr / ₹50 cr; Medium: ₹50 cr / ₹250 cr).
- **FDI** has two routes: **Automatic** (no approval) and **Government** (DPIIT approval). **Defence, Insurance, Banking** capped at 74% automatic.
- **SEZ Act 2005** provides tax holidays for export-oriented units.
- **Make in India (2014)** + **Startup India (2016)** + **Digital India (2015)** = flagship reform trinity.
- **GST implemented on July 1, 2017** — replaced 17 central and state taxes.
- **IBC 2016** brought time-bound insolvency resolution.
- **NITI Aayog (2015)** replaced the **Planning Commission**.
- **Atmanirbhar Bharat (2020)** + **PLI Scheme** (14 sectors, ₹1.97 lakh crore) = post-COVID reform strategy.
- **Amrit Kaal (2022–2047)** is the 25-year roadmap to **India@100**.
- **Cooperative Sector** — Amul (Dr. Verghese Kurien), IFFCO, KRIBHCO.
- **Public Sector** — BHEL, SAIL, ONGC; classified as **Maharatna, Navratna, Miniratna**.
- **Joint Sector** — PPP model; examples: Maruti Suzuki (originally), Delhi Metro.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Mantra:** Always write the Indian Scenario answer as a **chronology with cause-effect linkages**. Memorize the policy years (1948, 1956, 1977, 1980, 1991) as anchor dates. Use the **LPG triad** in correct order. Always cite **one real-world example** per sub-part. End with a **forward-looking statement** about the **Amrit Kaal** vision to impress the examiner.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Skill India
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET834 - BUSINESS ORGANIZATION AND DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Skill India

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview of Skill India

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Skill India** is a flagship national mission of the Government of India launched on **15 July 2015** (World Youth Skills Day) by the **Ministry of Skill Development and Entrepreneurship (MSDE)**. It is a comprehensive umbrella campaign designed to create an empowered workforce by skilling, upskilling, and reskilling the Indian youth, with a stated target of training **40 crore (400 million) people across diverse industry sectors by the year 2025**.

The mission operates under the foundational framework of the **National Policy for Skill Development and Entrepreneurship 2015** and is implemented through a coordinated network of **Central Government Ministries, State Governments, Sector Skill Councils (SSCs), industry partners, training providers, and academic institutions**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Statistic to Memorize for KTU Exams:**
> - **Target Beneficiaries:** 40 crore (400 million) Indians
> - **Target Year:** 2025
> - **Launch Date:** 15 July 2015
> - **Nodal Ministry:** Ministry of Skill Development and Entrepreneurship (MSDE)
> - **Foundational Policy Document:** National Policy for Skill Development and Entrepreneurship 2015

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Explanation

Think of **Skill India as a massive "national upskilling gym"** for the country. Imagine India as a giant factory with billions of workers (the population), but many workers are using outdated tools or no tools at all (lacking employable skills). The Skill India mission is the government's program to:

- **Open Gym Memberships** → Free/affordable training programs (PMKVY, NAPS)
- **Hire Trainers** → Create a national pool of certified trainers and assessors
- **Build Gym Branches Everywhere** → Establish **Pradhan Mantri Kaushal Kendras (PMKKs)** in every district
- **Issue Certificates Recognized Nationwide** → Standardized **National Skills Qualifications Framework (NSQF)** aligned certificates
- **Connect Members to Jobs** → Tie-ups with industry for **placement and apprenticeship** opportunities

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Skill India matters from a Business Organization perspective:**
> - Addresses the **demand–supply gap** in the labour market.
> - Reduces **structural unemployment** and underemployment.
> - Enhances **industry readiness** of the workforce.
> - Supports the **Make in India** and **Atmanirbhar Bharat** initiatives by ensuring skilled manpower.
> - Improves **productivity, employability, and entrepreneurship** at the grassroots level.

## 1.3 Core Objectives of Skill India

The mission is built around five interlocking objectives:

| # | Objective | Plain English Meaning |
|---|-----------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Create an empowered workforce** | Make Indians job-ready |
| 2 | **Skill, upskill, and reskill** | Train, retrain, and upgrade |
| 3 | **Promote entrepreneurship** | Help people start their own ventures |
| 4 | **Bridge the industry–academia gap** | Match what colleges teach with what companies need |
| 5 | **Inclusivity** | Focus on women, rural youth, SC/ST, and PwD (Persons with Disabilities) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis — Architecture & Components of Skill India

## 2.1 The Three-Tier Institutional Architecture

The Skill India mission operates through a **three-tier governance structure** that ensures policy formulation at the top, coordination at the middle, and execution at the grassroots.

### **Tier 1: Policy & Apex Body**
- **Prime Minister's Skill Development Council (PM Council)** – Chaired by the Hon'ble PM, sets the national skilling agenda.
- **National Skill Development Mission (NSDM)** – Launched on 15 July 2015; the apex coordinating body.
- **Ministry of Skill Development and Entrepreneurship (MSDE)** – The nodal ministry headquartered in New Delhi.

### **Tier 2: Coordination & Standardization**
- **National Skill Development Corporation (NSDC)** – A public-private partnership (PPP) body that funds and supports skill training.
- **National Skill Development Agency (NSDA)** – Oversees the **National Skills Qualifications Framework (NSQF)**.
- **Sector Skill Councils (SSCs)** – Currently **38 SSCs** representing different industries (e.g., Automotive SSC, IT-ITeS SSC, Healthcare SSC, Textile SSC).
- **National Skill Development Fund (NSDF)** – The financial backbone, funded by government budgetary allocations and corporate CSR contributions.

### **Tier 3: Execution & Delivery**
- **Pradhan Mantri Kaushal Kendras (PMKKs)** – District-level skill training centres.
- **Training Partners (TPs)** – Empanelled private and government training organizations.
- **Industry Partners** – Provide apprenticeship, internships, and placements.
- **Skill India Digital (SID)** – A unified digital platform for skilling, credentialing, and career services.

## 2.2 Major Flagship Schemes under Skill India

The Skill India umbrella covers more than **40+ central schemes and sub-schemes**. The most important ones for KTU examinations are:

### **A. Pradhan Mantri Kaushal Vikas Yojana (PMKVY)**
- **Full form:** Prime Minister's Skill Development Scheme
- **Launched:** 2015 (PMKVY 1.0), with subsequent versions — **PMKVY 2.0 (2016)**, **PMKVY 3.0 (2020)**, **PMKVY 4.0 (2022)**
- **Objective:** Enable Indian youth to take up industry-relevant skill training that will help them secure better livelihoods.
- **Two Pillars:**
  - **Short-Term Training (STT)** — 200 to 600 hours of training in specific job roles.
  - **Recognition of Prior Learning (RPL)** — Certifies the skills of workers who already have informal experience (e.g., a self-taught electrician).
- **Benefit:** Trainees receive a **government-recognized certificate** and a **placement opportunity**; training is **free** for short-term courses.

### **B. Pradhan Mantri Kaushal Kendra (PMKK)**
- **Concept:** "Skill India ka Mana" (The Soul of Skill India)
- **Aim:** Set up a **model skill training centre in every district** of India.
- **Role:** Acts as a one-stop centre for skilling, employer connect, and apprenticeship facilitation.

### **C. National Apprenticeship Promotion Scheme (NAPS)**
- **Objective:** Promote apprenticeship across all sectors by providing financial support to establishments that hire apprentices.
- **Benefit:** Apprentices receive a **stipend** (partly funded by the government), and establishments get **reimbursement** of stipend costs.
- **Coverage:** Both **Designated Trades** and **Optional Trades**.

### **D. Skills Strengthening for Industrial Value Enhancement (STRIVE)**
- **Aim:** Improve the quality and relevance of skill development programs through industry partnerships and IT enablement.
- **Funding:** World Bank-assisted project.

### **E. Skill India Digital (SID) / Skill India Digital Hub**
- **Launched:** 2023
- **Concept:** A unified digital ecosystem for skilling that integrates **Skilling, Credentialing, Career, and E-commerce** into one platform.
- **Features:** Verified digital credentials, AI-based job matching, e-learning content, entrepreneurship support.

### **F. Other Notable Schemes**
| Scheme | Focus Area |
|--------|------------|
| **Udaan** | Skill training for youth in Jammu & Kashmir |
| **Naya Savera** | Skilling for minorities |
| **Polytechnic scheme** | Industry-integrated diploma programs |
| **SC/ST skill development** | Inclusive skill training for marginalized communities |
| **Women empowerment programs** | Skill training specifically for women (e.g., Women in IT) |
| **PANKH** | Skilling for PwD (Persons with Disabilities) |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Summary Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Must-remember Components, Years, and Bodies for KTU 2024 Exams**

| Component | Year / Number | Significance |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|
| Skill India Launch | **15 July 2015** | Birth of the mission |
| Target Beneficiaries | **40 crore** | By 2025 |
| Nodal Ministry | **MSDE** | Policy & implementation |
| Apex Policy Document | **NPSD&E 2015** | National Policy for Skill Development and Entrepreneurship |
| Standardization Body | **NSQF** | National Skills Qualifications Framework (10 levels) |
| Public-Private Body | **NSDC** | Funding and partnership |
| Sector Skill Councils | **38 SSCs** | Industry-aligned curriculum design |
| District Centres | **PMKK** | One in every district |
| Stipend Scheme | **NAPS** | Apprenticeship promotion |
| Digital Platform | **Skill India Digital** | Unified digital ecosystem |
| Inclusive Schemes | **PANKH, Udaan, Naya Savera** | Marginalized groups |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility and Importance

- **For Business Managers:** Skill India is a **critical human capital pipeline** — companies in India (manufacturing, IT, retail) directly benefit from a more employable workforce, reducing their training costs.
- **For Entrepreneurs:** Initiatives like **PMKVY-RPL** certify informal workers, which helps entrepreneurs standardize their hiring and quality.
- **For the Economy:** Contributes to **GDP growth** (estimated 0.5–1% additional GDP per year), reduces **poverty**, and supports the vision of a **$5 trillion economy**.
- **For Students:** Direct entry-points to industry via **apprenticeship → placement** pathways reduce the gap between education and employment.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Analytical Matrix — Mission Framework, Pillars, and Critical Analysis

## 3.1 The Seven Pillars of the National Skill Development Mission (NSDM)

The mission is structured around **seven interconnected pillars**. KTU examiners frequently test a student's ability to list and explain these pillars in short-answer questions.

### **Pillar 1: Institutional Structure & Governance**
- Creation of **MSDE** as the apex nodal ministry.
- Formation of a **National Mission Directorate** under the Principal Secretary to the PM.
- Coordination across **20+ central ministries** involved in skilling.

### **Pillar 2: Capacity Building**
- Training of **trainers (ToT)** and **assessors (ToA)**.
- Setting up **Centres of Excellence (CoEs)** for advanced skilling in emerging technologies.
- Strengthening the **instructor ecosystem** in ITIs and polytechnics.

### **Pillar 3: Industry Engagement & Employer Connect**
- Active involvement of **Sector Skill Councils (SSCs)**.
- Promotion of **industry-defined NOS (National Occupational Standards)** and **QP (Qualification Packs)**.
- Industry sponsorships, CSR funding, and apprenticeship incentives.

### **Pillar 4: Quality Assurance**
- **NSQF (National Skills Qualifications Framework)** with 10 levels ensures comparability of certifications.
- **Third-party assessment** by independent certifying bodies (e.g., SSC-affiliated Assessment Agencies).
- **Skill India Digital (SID)** provides blockchain-based tamper-proof credentials.

### **Pillar 5: Convergence**
- Integration of skill training with existing welfare schemes (e.g., **MGNREGA**, **NRLM — National Rural Livelihoods Mission**).
- Convergence between **school education (under NEP 2020)**, **higher education**, and **vocational training**.

### **Pillar 6: International Partnerships**
- Bilateral agreements with countries like **Germany, Japan, UK, Australia, UAE** for global skill benchmarking.
- Migration of Indian skilled workers to **Gulf countries, Europe, and North America** under mutually recognized certifications.
- India is an active member of the **G20 Skills Alliance**.

### **Pillar 7: Skill for All — Inclusivity**
- Special focus on **women** (target: 30% female participation in PMKVY).
- Outreach to **rural youth, SC/ST, OBC, EWS**, and **PwD (Persons with Disabilities)**.
- Mobile-based skilling vans for remote and tribal areas.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Analytical Framework — How Skill India Works in Practice

To understand the operational mechanics, here is the **end-to-end skill training flow** under PMKVY:

**Step 1 — Demand Aggregation**
- The mission begins with **identifying skill demand** through **Skill Gap Studies** conducted by NSDC and SSCs.
- **Industries and governments** project future workforce requirements across sectors (e.g., 5 lakh electricians needed by 2027).

**Step 2 — Job Role Definition**
- Sector Skill Councils develop **National Occupational Standards (NOS)** and **Qualification Packs (QP)** for each role.
- These documents define what a candidate **must know and must do** to be employable.

**Step 3 — Curriculum Design**
- **NSQF-aligned** curriculum is created.
- Courses are mapped to **NSQF Levels 3 to 7** (typically — Level 1 is unskilled, Level 10 is doctoral-level).

**Step 4 — Training Partner Empanelment**
- The **NSDC** empanels **Training Partners (TPs)** — both government and private — based on infrastructure, trainer quality, and historical performance.
- TPs are graded as **A, B, or C** based on outcomes.

**Step 5 — Mobilization of Candidates**
- Candidates are mobilized through **Skill Saathi** (a dedicated app), **Common Service Centres (CSCs)**, **PMKKs**, and grassroots organizations.
- **Aadhaar-based** registration ensures transparency.

**Step 6 — Training Delivery**
- Trainees undergo **200 to 600 hours** of theory + practical training.
- Trainers use a **blended learning** model (classroom + digital + on-the-job).
- **Aadhaar-linked biometric attendance** ensures genuine participation.

**Step 7 — Assessment & Certification**
- Trainees are assessed by **independent assessment agencies** (not the training partner itself, ensuring impartiality).
- Successful candidates receive **NSQF-aligned government-recognized certificates** — either in physical or digital form via the **Skill India Digital (SID)** wallet.

**Step 8 — Placement & Apprenticeship Linkage**
- The TP is mandated to provide **placement support** to at least **70% of certified candidates**.
- NAPS and direct industry tie-ups convert candidates into apprentices or full-time employees.
- Post-placement tracking is done for **6 months** to verify the quality of the placement.

**Step 9 — Career Progression & Lifelong Learning**
- The mission envisions a **"Skilling → Working → Upskilling"** life cycle.
- SID platform allows candidates to **stack credentials** (micro-credentials) and continuously upgrade skills.

## 3.3 Critical Analysis — Strengths and Challenges

### **Strengths**
- **Scale:** Largest skilling mission in the world by target population.
- **Standardization:** NSQF provides a nationally and internationally recognized framework.
- **PPP model:** Efficient use of private sector innovation and infrastructure.
- **Digital transformation:** SID is among the most advanced digital skilling platforms globally.
- **Inclusivity:** Specific focus on marginalized communities.

### **Challenges**
- **Quality vs. Quantity Tension:** Pressure to meet 40 crore target sometimes leads to rushed certifications.
- **Placement Quality:** Many certified candidates are placed in low-wage informal jobs.
- **Trainer Quality:** Shortage of industry-experienced trainers in emerging technologies (AI, robotics, green energy).
- **Rural-Urban Divide:** Skilling infrastructure is concentrated in urban areas.
- **Informal Sector Coverage:** Despite RPL, a large informal workforce remains un-served.

## 3.4 KTU Analytical Matrix — Connecting Schemes to Outcomes

| Scheme | Target Group | Skill Type | Expected Outcome |
|--------|--------------|------------|------------------|
| PMKVY-STT | School/college dropouts | Entry-level job skills | Wage employment |
| PMKVY-RPL | Existing informal workers | Certification of existing skills | Formalization, better wages |
| NAPS | All youth (18+) | Apprenticeship-based | Skilled workforce + income |
| PMKK | District-level aspirants | Multi-sector skilling | Local employment generation |
| STRIVE | Industrial workforce | Advanced/upskilling | Industry 4.0 readiness |
| Skill India Digital | All citizens | Digital & lifelong learning | Continuous upskilling |
| Udaan | J&K youth | Multi-sector | Inclusive growth |
| PANKH | PwD | Adapted skill training | Empowerment of PwD |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Examiners often frame questions on **"How does Skill India contribute to economic development?"** Use the framework above: **employment generation → productivity → GDP contribution → poverty reduction → demographic dividend utilization**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Skill India. State any two of its key objectives.**
**Model Answer:**
Skill India is a flagship national mission launched on **15 July 2015** by the Ministry of Skill Development and Entrepreneurship (MSDE) to train over **40 crore Indians** in industry-relevant skills by 2025.

**Two Key Objectives:**
1. **Skill, upskill, and reskill** the Indian workforce to make them employable and globally competitive.
2. **Promote entrepreneurship** by enabling trained youth to start their own ventures.

### **Q2. What is the National Skills Qualifications Framework (NSQF)?**
**Model Answer:**
The **NSQF** is a competency-based framework that organizes qualifications into **10 levels**, each described in terms of learning outcomes (knowledge, skills, and attitudes). It ensures that all skill certifications issued under Skill India are **nationally comparable, internationally recognized, and aligned with industry needs**, enabling horizontal and vertical mobility for learners.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - ❌ Do **NOT** write generic essays on "Skill Development in India" — anchor your answer to the **official mission name, date, ministry, and schemes**.
> - ❌ Avoid listing schemes without explaining their **purpose and target group**.
> - ✅ Always mention **specific numbers** (40 crore target, 38 SSCs, NSQF 10 levels) — examiners award marks for **data-driven answers**.
> - ✅ Use **headings, sub-points, and diagrams** for 14-mark answers — KTU follows a structured valuation grid.
> - ✅ Conclude with the **macro-impact** (GDP, employment, Atmanirbhar Bharat).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** *Discuss the major schemes and components of the Skill India mission. Explain how it contributes to economic development.*

**Model Solution Outline:**

**Part (a) — Major Schemes of Skill India (7 Marks)**

1. **Pradhan Mantri Kaushal Vikas Yojana (PMKVY)** — flagship short-term training scheme, with versions 1.0 to 4.0; offers free training + placement support.
2. **Pradhan Mantri Kaushal Kendra (PMKK)** — district-level model training centres; "the soul of Skill India."
3. **National Apprenticeship Promotion Scheme (NAPS)** — incentivizes establishments to take apprentices; reimburses part of stipend.
4. **Skills Strengthening for Industrial Value Enhancement (STRIVE)** — World Bank-assisted; focuses on industry 4.0 skills.
5. **Skill India Digital (SID)** — unified digital platform for skilling, credentialing, and career services (launched 2023).
6. **Specialized Inclusive Schemes** — Udaan (J&K), Naya Savera (minorities), PANKH (PwD).

**Part (b) — Contribution to Economic Development (7 Marks)**

1. **Employment Generation:** Reduces unemployment and underemployment, especially among youth.
2. **Productivity Enhancement:** Skilled workers are more productive, increasing industrial output.
3. **Demographic Dividend Utilization:** Converts India's young population into a productive workforce.
4. **Supports Make in India & Atmanirbhar Bharat:** Provides skilled manpower for domestic manufacturing and reduces foreign dependence.
5. **Entrepreneurship Promotion:** Encourages self-employment and MSME growth.
6. **Reduction in Poverty & Inequality:** Provides income opportunities to marginalized groups.
7. **GDP Contribution:** Estimated to add **0.5–1% to annual GDP** through productivity gains and employment.

**Conclusion:** Skill India is a transformative mission that integrates human capital development with India's broader economic goals, positioning India as a global skill hub.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** *Explain the institutional architecture and operational framework of the Skill India mission. How does the seven-pillar model ensure effective implementation?*

**Model Solution Outline:**

**Part (a) — Institutional Architecture (7 Marks)**

**Tier 1 (Apex):**
- Prime Minister's Skill Development Council
- National Skill Development Mission (NSDM)
- Ministry of Skill Development and Entrepreneurship (MSDE)

**Tier 2 (Coordination):**
- National Skill Development Corporation (NSDC) — PPP body for funding
- National Skill Development Agency (NSDA) — NSQF oversight
- **38 Sector Skill Councils (SSCs)** — industry-aligned standards
- National Skill Development Fund (NSDF) — financial backbone

**Tier 3 (Execution):**
- PMKKs, Training Partners, Industry Partners, Skill India Digital platform

**Part (b) — Seven Pillar Model for Effective Implementation (7 Marks)**

1. **Institutional Governance** — clear hierarchy and accountability
2. **Capacity Building** — trainer and assessor development
3. **Industry Engagement** — employer-driven curriculum
4. **Quality Assurance (NSQF)** — standardized certifications
5. **Convergence** — integration with welfare and education schemes
6. **International Partnerships** — global recognition and migration support
7. **Inclusivity (Skill for All)** — women, rural, SC/ST, PwD focus

**Conclusion:** The three-tier governance + seven-pillar framework creates a robust, accountable, and industry-aligned system that distinguishes Skill India from fragmented pre-2015 skilling efforts.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Launch Date:** Skill India was launched on **15 July 2015**.
- ✅ **Target:** Train **40 crore (400 million)** Indians by 2025.
- ✅ **Nodal Ministry:** **Ministry of Skill Development and Entrepreneurship (MSDE)**.
- ✅ **Foundational Policy:** **National Policy for Skill Development and Entrepreneurship 2015**.
- ✅ **Standardization:** **NSQF (National Skills Qualifications Framework)** with **10 levels**.
- ✅ **Sector Skill Councils:** **38 SSCs** representing various industries.
- ✅ **Key Schemes:** **PMKVY, PMKK, NAPS, STRIVE, Skill India Digital, Udaan, Naya Savera, PANKH**.
- ✅ **PMKVY Versions:** 1.0 (2015), 2.0 (2016), 3.0 (2020), 4.0 (2022).
- ✅ **Two Pillars of PMKVY:** Short-Term Training (STT) and Recognition of Prior Learning (RPL).
- ✅ **Skill India Digital (SID):** Launched in 2023; integrates skilling, credentialing, career, and e-commerce.
- ✅ **Three-Tier Architecture:** Apex (MSDE/NSDM) → Coordination (NSDC/SSCs) → Execution (PMKKs/TPs).
- ✅ **Seven Pillars of NSDM:** Institutional structure, Capacity building, Industry engagement, Quality assurance, Convergence, International partnerships, Inclusivity.
- ✅ **Apprenticeship Scheme:** **NAPS** reimburses 25% of apprenticeship stipend (for establishments with up to 500 workers).
- ✅ **Macro-impact:** Skill India supports **Make in India, Atmanirbhar Bharat, and the $5 trillion economy vision**.
- ✅ **For exam writing:** Always cite **specific numbers, dates, scheme names, and target groups** — this is the KTU valuation pattern.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Revision Tip:** Before the exam, practice drawing the **three-tier architecture diagram** and the **PMKVY workflow diagram** from Section 4. KTU examiners award up to **2–3 marks** for well-drawn, correctly labeled diagrams in 14-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Make in India
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET834 - BUSINESS ORGANIZATION AND DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Make in India

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Make in India** is a flagship national initiative launched by the **Government of India** on **25 September 2014** by the Honourable Prime Minister Shri Narendra Modi. The programme is designed to **transform India into a global manufacturing hub** by encouraging companies—domestic and multinational—to design, develop, manufacture, and assemble their products within the country, thereby accelerating economic growth, generating employment, and enhancing the global competitiveness of Indian industries.

The initiative is administered by the **Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT)**, which falls under the **Ministry of Commerce and Industry**. It is one of the four flagship programmes of the Government under the broader **"Skill India," "Digital India," and "Stand Up India"** framework.

### Formal KTU Definition (Exam-Ready)

> [!NOTE]
> *"Make in India is a Government of India initiative launched on 25th September 2014 to facilitate investment, foster innovation, enhance skill development, protect intellectual property, and build best-in-class manufacturing infrastructure in India, with the objective of making India a global manufacturing destination."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine India as a **giant workshop** that for decades primarily exported raw materials (cotton, iron ore, minerals) and imported finished goods (cars, electronics, machinery). The country earned very little because the *value addition* happened elsewhere.

**"Make in India" is like setting up a massive, well-equipped factory inside this workshop**—with:
- **Cheap, skilled labour** (the workforce)
- **Modern machinery** (technology & FDI)
- **Easy permits** (single-window clearances)
- **High-quality raw materials** (domestic resources)
- **Reliable electricity, roads, and ports** (infrastructure)

So that the **entire product**—from raw material to finished good—is **made inside India**, capturing the full profit, employment, and innovation value.

> [!TIP]
> **One-Line Memory Hook for Exam:**
> *"Make in India = Manufacture in India + Invest in India + Innovate in India."*

---

## 1.3 Key Statistics & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Value / Metric |
|---|---|
| **Launch Date** | 25 September 2014 |
| **Launched By** | PM Narendra Modi (from Vigyan Bhawan, New Delhi) |
| **Nodal Body** | Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT) |
| **Parent Ministry** | Ministry of Commerce and Industry |
| **Initial Sectors Identified** | **25 sectors** |
| **Current Focus Sectors** | 27+ sectors (expanded over time) |
| **Tagline** | "Make in India – Made in India – Made for the World" |
| **Logo Symbol** | A lion made of cogs, symbolising strength and manufacturing |

---

## 1.4 Why "Make in India" Was Conceptualised

Before 2014, India's manufacturing sector's contribution to **GDP was stagnant at around 14–16%**, far below the target of **25%** envisioned by the **National Manufacturing Policy (2011)**. India's neighbour **China had over 30%** of its GDP coming from manufacturing.

The slow growth was attributed to:
- Complex labour laws
- Poor infrastructure
- Low Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) inflows
- Bureaucratic delays in approvals
- Difficulty in doing business

> [!NOTE]
> **India's Rank in Ease of Doing Business (pre-initiative):** **142nd** out of 189 countries (World Bank, 2014).
> **India's Rank in Ease of Doing Business (latest):** **63rd** (2020 report) — a dramatic improvement of 79 ranks in 5 years, partly attributed to reforms under Make in India.

---

## 1.5 Vision & Mission

### Vision
> To **make India the world's top manufacturing destination** by 2025 (later revised).

### Mission
1. Boost manufacturing sector's GDP share to **25%**.
2. Create **100 million additional jobs** by 2022.
3. Increase the contribution of manufacturing to India's exports.
4. Develop India as a **knowledge-based economy**.
5. Reduce dependence on imports and promote **self-reliance (Aatmanirbhar Bharat)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Sheet

## 2.1 The Nine Pillars of Make in India

The initiative rests on **nine strategic pillars** (also known as the *9-Pillar Framework*). These form the theoretical backbone of the programme and are **highly important for KTU 14-mark questions**.

| # | Pillar | Description | Engineering / Business Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Ease of Doing Business** | Single-window clearances, online approvals, reduced compliance burden | Reduces time-to-market for entrepreneurs |
| 2 | **Foreign Direct Investment (FDI)** | Liberalised FDI norms across sectors | Brings capital, technology, global best practices |
| 3 | **Industrial Corridor** | Dedicated manufacturing corridors with world-class infrastructure | Plug-and-play industrial estates |
| 4 | **Innovation and R&D** | Promotion of IP rights, Atal Innovation Mission, incubators | Builds indigenous technology base |
| 5 | **Skill Development** | Skill India Mission, PMKVY, ITI upgradation | Bridges the industry–academia gap |
| 6 | **MSME Promotion** | Easy credit, MUDRA loans, technology centres | Strengthens small-scale industries |
| 7 | **Infrastructure** | Sagarmala, Bharatmala, Sagarmala, Smart Cities, UDAN | Logistics efficiency for supply chain |
| 8 | **Trade Facilitation** | Digital customs, GST integration, logistics cost reduction | Boosts export competitiveness |
| 9 | **Clean Energy & Sustainability** | National Solar Mission, Electric Mobility, green manufacturing | Aligns with global ESG standards |

---

## 2.2 Strategic Sectors Identified (Original 25 Sectors)

The initiative initially identified **25 priority sectors** for focused intervention. Understanding these sectors is essential because KTU questions often ask: *"List the major sectors under Make in India"* or *"Discuss any five sectors."*

### Original 25 Sectors:
1. Automobiles
2. Auto Components
3. Aviation
4. Biotechnology
5. Chemicals
6. Construction
7. Defence Manufacturing
8. Electrical Machinery
9. Electronic Systems Design & Manufacturing (ESDM)
10. Food Processing
11. Gems & Jewellery
12. IT & BPM
13. Leather
14. Media & Entertainment
15. Mining
16. Oil & Gas
17. Pharmaceuticals
17. Ports
18. Railways
19. Renewable Energy
20. Roads & Highways
21. Space
22. Textiles & Garments
23. Thermal Power
24. Tourism & Hospitality
25. Wellness

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **New Sectors Added Later (after 2020):** Toy manufacturing, **semiconductors and electronics** (under the India Semiconductor Mission), drones, PLI (Production Linked Incentive) sectors, Green Hydrogen, Advanced Chemistry Cell (ACC) batteries, and Telecom.

---

## 2.3 Key Reforms Triggered by Make in India

### A. Ease of Doing Business Reforms
- **Single-window clearance system**: A single portal for all approvals.
- **Online Licensing**: e-Biz platform integrating 14 Central Government services.
- **Elimination of inspector raj**: Random inspections replaced with self-certification.
- **Reduction in number of compliances**: From over 2,500 to under 1,300 in many states.
- **Time-bound approvals**: Maximum 30 days for clearances in many states.

### B. FDI Liberalisation
Major sectors opened to **100% automatic FDI route**:
- Defence (up to 74% automatic, beyond via government route)
- Railways infrastructure
- Single-brand retail
- Construction
- Pharmaceuticals
- Insurance (up to 74%)
- Telecom

> [!NOTE]
> **FDI Inflow Comparison:**
> $$FDI_{2013-14} \approx \text{USD 36 billion}$$
> $$FDI_{2023-24} \approx \text{USD 71 billion (cumulative inflows during Make in India era)}$$

### C. Industrial Corridor Projects
The **National Industrial Corridor Development Programme (NICDP)** aims to create futuristic, world-class industrial cities.
- **Delhi-Mumbai Industrial Corridor (DMIC)** — the flagship corridor
- Chennai-Bengaluru Industrial Corridor
- Amritsar-Kolkata Industrial Corridor
- Bengaluru-Mumbai Economic Corridor
- Hyderabad-Warangal Industrial Corridor

Each corridor includes:
- Smart industrial cities
- Plug-and-play factories
- Dedicated freight corridors
- Logistics parks
- Cold-chain infrastructure

### D. Intellectual Property Rights (IPR)
- **National Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) Policy, 2016** launched.
- Commercialisation of IP framework established.
- **Bharat Patent** — for grassroots innovations.
- Reduced patent filing time from 5–7 years to under 18 months.

---

## 2.4 Production Linked Incentive (PLI) Scheme — The Modern Avatar

Launched in **March 2020** (initially for 3 sectors, expanded to 14+), the PLI scheme is the **successor / extension** of Make in India and is **frequently asked in KTU exams**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU exam-ready):**
> *"The Production Linked Incentive (PLI) Scheme is a government financial incentive programme that rewards manufacturers for incremental sales of specified products manufactured in India, with the objective of boosting domestic manufacturing and making Indian manufacturers globally competitive."*

### Current PLI Sectors (14):
1. Mobile manufacturing & specified electronic components
2. Critical Key Starting Materials (KSMs)/Drug Intermediates & APIs
3. Manufacturing of Medical Devices
4. Automobiles & Auto Components
5. Pharmaceuticals Drugs
6. Specialty Steel
7. Telecom & Networking Products
8. Electronic/Technology Products
9. White Goods (ACs & LED)
10. Food Processing Industry
11. High-Efficiency Solar PV Modules
12. Advanced Chemistry Cell (ACC) Battery
13. Textile Products (MMF, technical textiles)
14. Drones & Drone Components

### PLI Financial Outlay
$$\text{Total Outlay} = \text{Rs. 1.97 lakh crore (approx.)}$$

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (For Numerical / Analytical Questions)

Although "Make in India" is a qualitative initiative, certain analytical calculations are sometimes asked in KTU exams:

| Parameter | Formula | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Sector Growth Rate (CAGR) | $CAGR = \left(\dfrac{V_f}{V_i}\right)^{\frac{1}{n}} - 1$ | Measures compounded annual growth rate of manufacturing sector |
| FDI Inflow Change | $\Delta FDI (\%) = \dfrac{FDI_{new} - FDI_{old}}{FDI_{old}} \times 100$ | Measures year-on-year growth in FDI |
| Manufacturing's GDP Share | $Share_M = \dfrac{GDP_{manufacturing}}{GDP_{total}} \times 100$ | Target: 25% |
| Employment Multiplier | $E_M = \dfrac{\Delta Employment}{\Delta Investment}$ | Jobs created per crore invested |
| Effective Tax Rate for New Manufacturing | $E_t = 15\%$ (for new companies) | Under Sec 115BAB, Income Tax Act |
| Cost Savings under PLI | $Savings = Incentive\% \times Incremental Sales$ | Variable subsidy based on output |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility for Engineers (OEMET834 Perspective)

For B.Tech students, the relevance of Make in India extends beyond theory:

- **Employment generation**: New manufacturing plants need **engineers** (mechanical, electrical, electronics, civil, computer science).
- **Startup ecosystem**: Initiatives like **Atal Innovation Mission (AIM)** and **TIE** empower tech entrepreneurs.
- **Research opportunities**: Funding for R&D in semiconductors, EVs, drones, robotics.
- **Internships and placements**: Companies like Apple, Samsung, Foxconn, Tesla are setting up Indian facilities.
- **Skill alignment**: KTU curriculum aligned with industry needs via NEP 2020.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analysis & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Framework: Causal Chain of "Make in India"

To secure full marks in KTU 14-mark questions, students should present the **causal logic chain** that connects the initiative to its outcomes. Below is the step-by-step analytical model:

### Step 1: Identify the Problem (Pre-2014 Status)

$$\text{Manufacturing GDP share} \approx 14\% \text{ to } 16\%$$

$$\text{Ease of Doing Business Rank}_{2014} = 142 / 189$$

$$\text{FDI Inflows (2013-14)} \approx \text{USD 36 billion}$$

$$\text{Manufacturing Employment Share} \approx 12\% \text{ of workforce}$$

### Step 2: Define the Strategic Objective

$$\text{Target: } Manufacturing_{GDP\,share} \to 25\%$$

$$\text{Target: } 100 \text{ million new jobs by 2025}$$

$$\text{Target: } Top\ 50 \text{ Global Manufacturing Hub}$$

### Step 3: Design the Intervention (9 Pillars)

$$\text{Reforms} = f(\text{EODB},\ \text{FDI},\ \text{Infrastructure},\ \text{Skill},\ \text{MSME},\ \text{Innovation},\ \text{Corridors},\ \text{Trade},\ \text{Sustainability})$$

### Step 4: Deploy the Intervention (Sectors + Schemes)

Each of the **25 (now 27+) sectors** gets a sector-specific action plan. Example for **Electronics System Design & Manufacturing (ESDM):**
- Modified Special Incentive Package Scheme (M-SIPS)
- Electronic Manufacturing Clusters (EMCs)
- Phased Manufacturing Programme (PMP)
- PLI for mobile & components

### Step 5: Measure the Outcome

$$\Delta Y = Y_{after} - Y_{before}$$

For example:

$$\Delta Manufacturing_{GDP} = 16\% \to 14\% \to 17\% \text{ (estimated for 2023-24)}$$

### Step 6: Feedback Loop for Continuous Improvement

$$\text{Performance Review} \Rightarrow \text{Policy Modification} \Rightarrow \text{Re-implementation}$$

This is why we have:
- **Make in India (2014)** → **PLI (2020)** → **Aatmanirbhar Bharat (2020)** → **India Semiconductor Mission (2021)**.

---

## 3.2 Comparative Analytical Matrix (Before vs After)

| Dimension | Pre-Make in India (Pre-2014) | Post-Make in India (2014–2024) | Improvement (%) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ease of Doing Business Rank | 142 (2014) | 63 (2020) | ↑ **55.6%** |
| FDI Inflows (Annual) | USD 36 Bn (2013-14) | USD 71 Bn (2021-22) | ↑ **97.2%** |
| Mobile Phone Production | 60 million units (2014) | 330+ million units (2022) | ↑ **450%** |
| Mobile Exports | ~Rs. 1,500 Cr (2014) | ~Rs. 45,000 Cr (2022) | ↑ **2,900%** |
| Defence Production (India) | ~Rs. 52,000 Cr (2014) | ~Rs. 1,06,800 Cr (2022) | ↑ **105%** |
| Manufacturing Share in GDP | 16.3% | 14–17% (volatile) | Mixed |
| Patents Filed | 42,763 (2014-15) | 66,440 (2022-23) | ↑ **55.4%** |
| Startup Ecosystem | ~400 recognised | 1,17,000+ (2023) | ↑ **291x** |

---

## 3.3 Sector-Wise Analytical Deep-Dive (Top 5 Sectors)

### Sector A: Automobile & Auto Components
- India is the **4th largest automotive market** globally and the **2nd largest two-wheeler manufacturer**.
- Major investments: Tata Motors (EV plant), Hyundai (Tamil Nadu), Maruti Suzuki (Gujarat).
- PLI outlay: **Rs. 25,938 crore**.
- Target: Increase auto exports by **$20 billion by 2030**.

### Sector B: Electronics System Design & Manufacturing (ESDM)
- India is now the **2nd largest mobile phone manufacturer** globally (overtaking Vietnam in 2022).
- Apple now manufactures iPhones in India (Foxconn, Wistron facilities).
- PLI outlay: **Rs. 54,000+ crore**.
- Mobile phone imports reduced from **78% (2014) to under 5% (2023)** for feature phones.

### Sector C: Defence Manufacturing
- India was historically the **world's largest arms importer**.
- **Defence Production & Export Promotion Policy (DPEPP) 2020** introduced.
- Defence exports grew from **Rs. 1,521 Cr (2014) to Rs. 15,920 Cr (2022-23)**.
- Positive indigenisation lists released to ban import of 500+ items.

### Sector D: Pharmaceuticals
- India is the **"Pharmacy of the World"** — largest provider of generic medicines globally.
- PLI outlay: **Rs. 15,000 crore** for bulk drugs and APIs.
- Reduces import dependence (especially from China) for **Key Starting Materials (KSMs)**.

### Sector E: Renewable Energy
- Target: **500 GW renewable energy capacity by 2030**.
- India is the **4th globally in renewable energy installed capacity**.
- ISA (International Solar Alliance) headquartered in India — India emerging as a global climate leader.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Illustration (KTU Exam Style)

**Question:** *India's FDI inflows in 2013-14 were USD 36 billion. In 2022-23, the inflows were USD 71 billion. Calculate the percentage increase in FDI inflows.*

**Step 1:** Identify the values.
$$FDI_{old} = 36 \text{ billion USD}$$
$$FDI_{new} = 71 \text{ billion USD}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the percentage change formula.
$$\Delta FDI (\%) = \dfrac{FDI_{new} - FDI_{old}}{FDI_{old}} \times 100$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values.
$$\Delta FDI (\%) = \dfrac{71 - 36}{36} \times 100$$

$$\Delta FDI (\%) = \dfrac{35}{36} \times 100$$

$$\Delta FDI (\%) = 97.22\%$$

**Step 4:** Conclusion.
$$\boxed{\Delta FDI (\%) \approx 97.22\% \text{ increase in FDI inflows from 2013-14 to 2022-23.}}$$

This illustrates the substantial impact of reforms under Make in India in attracting foreign capital.

---

## 3.5 The "Make in India 2.0" Conceptual Evolution

A **theoretical extension** now taught in advanced B.Tech management courses (relevant for higher-order Bloom's questions):

| Phase | Period | Focus | Key Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Make in India 1.0** | 2014–2019 | Ease of doing business, FDI, infrastructure | Improved EoDB rank, FDI up |
| **Make in India 2.0** | 2020–Present | PLI, Aatmanirbhar Bharat, Self-reliance | Stronger domestic supply chains |
| **Make in India 3.0 (Future)** | 2025+ | Green manufacturing, AI-driven factories, Industry 5.0 | Global leadership in advanced manufacturing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Connection to Industry 4.0 / 5.0:**
> The next phase of Make in India is expected to leverage **Cyber-Physical Systems, IoT, AI, Additive Manufacturing, and Cobots** to build *smart factories*. This is a **direct KTU relevance** for engineering students.

---

## 3.6 Challenges and Critical Analysis (For Higher-Order Questions)

A complete KTU answer must include a **balanced view** of limitations:

### Key Challenges
1. **Manufacturing's GDP share stagnant**: Remains around 14–17%, far from the 25% target.
2. **Skill gap**: Despite Skill India, ~60% of engineers are deemed unemployable per NASSCOM reports.
3. **Logistics cost**: India's logistics cost is 14–16% of GDP vs China's 8–9%.
4. **MSME credit crunch**: Banks' reluctance to lend to small units persists.
5. **Land acquisition issues**: Industrial corridors face delays due to land disputes.
6. **Dependence on imports**: Still heavily dependent on China for KSMs, APIs, semiconductors.
7. **Quality standards**: Manufacturing quality in some sectors still below global benchmarks.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Common Pitfall:** Many students write only the *positives* of Make in India. KTU examiners reward **balanced analysis**. Always include at least **3 challenges** in your 14-mark answer.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Architecture of Make in India

The following block diagram maps the **operational architecture** of the Make in India initiative, showing how all 9 pillars, sectors, and outcomes interact.

```mermaid

    C --> C7[Other 19 Sectors]
```

## 4.2 Evolution Roadmap of Indian Manufacturing Policy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[National Manufacturing Policy 2011] --> P2[Make in India 2014]
    P2 --> P3[Skill India 2015]
    P2 --> P4[Demonetisation and GST 2016-17]
    P2 --> P5[PLI Scheme 2020]
    P4 --> P5

    P6 --> P7[India Semiconductor Mission 2021]
    P6 --> P8[PM Gati Shakti 2021]

```

## 4.3 Stakeholder Ecosystem of Make in India

```mermaid

    Central[Central Government DPIIT] --> State[State Governments]
    Central --> Industry[Industry and Manufacturers]

    Ancillary --> Growth

```

## 4.4 PLI Scheme Operational Flow


```mermaid
    Reforms --> Investment[Increased Domestic and FDI Investment]
```
