<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define Incorporation. State any two effects of incorporation under the Companies Act, 2013.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Incorporation is the legal process by which a company is registered under the Companies Act, 2013, with the Registrar of Companies (ROC), resulting in the creation of a separate legal entity distinct from its members.

**Two Effects of Incorporation:**

1. **Separate Legal Entity:** Once incorporated, the company becomes a distinct legal person, separate from its members and directors. It can own property, enter contracts, and sue or be sued in its own name. (*Salomon v. Salomon, 1897*)

2. **Perpetual Succession:** The company continues to exist irrespective of the death, insolvency, or change of its members. The shares may be transferred, but the company continues.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Distinguish between Memorandum of Association and Articles of Association.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | MOA | AOA |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Defines the **objects and powers** of the company | Defines the **rules for internal management** |
| Governed by | Section 4 | Section 5 |
| Doctrine | Ultra Vires | Constructive Notice & Indoor Management |
| Priority | Subordinate to Companies Act | Subordinate to Companies Act and MOA |

---

## 5.2 Part B Question Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the various stages of incorporation of a company under the Companies Act, 2013.** (7 marks)

**(b) What is a Pre-incorporation contract? Discuss the legal position regarding pre-incorporation contracts with reference to the case of Kelantan Coconut Co. v. National Union Plantation Co. (1955).** (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The incorporation of a company involves the following sequential stages:

**1. Promotion:**
The process begins with **promoters** identifying a business opportunity and conducting feasibility studies. Promoters arrange capital, prepare initial documents, and undertake preliminary actions. They are not the legal entity itself; they act in a fiduciary capacity.

**2. Drafting of Documents:**
Three key documents are prepared:
- **Memorandum of Association (MOA)** — Defines the company's objectives, scope, and powers
- **Articles of Association (AOA)** — Defines the internal rules and management structure
- **Declaration in INC-9** — Professional declaration of compliance

**3. Digital Signature Certificate (DSC):**
All proposed directors and subscribers obtain DSCs from Certifying Authorities to sign electronic forms filed with MCA.

**4. Director Identification Number (DIN):**
Every director applies for a unique 8-digit DIN under **Section 153** of the Companies Act, 2013.

**5. Name Reservation:**
Application for name approval is made through the **RUN (Reserve Unique Name)** service on the MCA portal. The name must comply with the naming guidelines and end with "Private Limited" or "Limited."

**6. Filing of SPICe+ Form (INC-32):**
A single integrated form is filed for name reservation, DIN application, incorporation, PAN, TAN, and optional GSTIN.

**7. Certificate of Incorporation:**
The ROC issues the **Certificate of Incorporation in Form INC-11** within 7 days if all requirements are complied with. This certificate serves as conclusive evidence of the company's existence and includes auto-generated PAN and TAN.

**8. Commencement of Business:**
The company must file **INC-20A** within 180 days to commence business and exercise borrowing powers.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Definition:**
A **pre-incorporation contract** is a contract entered into by the promoters on behalf of a company that has not yet come into legal existence at the time of contract formation.

**Legal Position (Kelantan Coconut Co. v. National Union Plantation Co., 1955):**

The Court of Appeal held that:
1. A company has **no legal existence** before incorporation.
2. Therefore, a **pre-incorporation contract is void ab initio** (void from the beginning).
3. The company **cannot ratify** such a contract after incorporation.
4. The company **cannot sue or be sued** on such a contract.
5. The **promoter is personally liable** on the contract.
6. The company is not liable as principal because it never existed when the contract was made.

**Remedies Available to Promoters:**

1. **Fresh Contract (Novation):** The company can enter into a new contract with the third party after incorporation.

2. **Indemnity:** A promoter can claim indemnity from co-promoters for any personal liability incurred.

3. **Expenses:** A promoter is entitled to recover reasonable preliminary expenses incurred in the formation of the company.

4. **Agency Theory:** If the promoter expressly acts as agent of a named principal (the future company), the third party may be estopped from claiming personal liability on the promoter.

**Conclusion:**
The legal position protects third parties who deal in good faith with promoters, ensuring that promoters cannot escape liability by claiming they acted "on behalf of a company" that did not yet exist.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a) Discuss the doctrine of Separate Legal Entity and Limited Liability with reference to the landmark case of Salomon v. Salomon & Co. Ltd. (1897).** (7 marks)

**(b) Explain the clauses of the Memorandum of Association. Also state the procedure for alteration of MOA under Section 13 of the Companies Act, 2013.** (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Doctrine of Separate Legal Entity:**
This doctrine establishes that once a company is incorporated, it becomes a legal person distinct from its members, directors, and shareholders. The company has its own rights, obligations, and liabilities separate from those of the individuals who own or manage it.

**Landmark Case: Salomon v. Salomon & Co. Ltd. (1897)**

**Facts:**
- Mr. Aron Salomon was a sole proprietor of a leather business.
- He incorporated a private limited company with himself, his wife, daughter, and four sons as subscribers.
- Out of 20,007 shares, Salomon held 20,001 shares; the remaining 6 were held by family members.
- He sold his business to the company for £39,000, taking 20,000 shares and a debenture of £10,000.
- The company went into liquidation; liabilities exceeded assets.
- The liquidator argued that Salomon and the company were the same entity, and therefore Salomon should be personally liable for the company's debts.

**Held by House of Lords:**
- The company was a **separate legal entity** distinct from its members, even when one person held virtually all the shares.
- The company was not a mere agent or alias of Salomon.
- Therefore, Salomon's liability was **limited to his unpaid subscription**, which was already paid.

**Doctrine of Limited Liability:**
The liability of a shareholder/member is **limited to the unpaid amount on the shares** held by them. If the shares are fully paid, the member has **no further liability** (except in cases of fraud or guarantee).

**Exceptions to Limited Liability:**
1. **Winding Up:** If the court finds the company was formed to defraud creditors.
2. **Holding-Subsidiary:** The holding company may be liable for subsidiary's acts in certain cases.
3. **Fraudulent Trading:** Under Section 339, members may be personally liable.
4. **Group of Companies:** Courts may lift the corporate veil in exceptional cases.

**Case Laws on Lifting the Corporate Veil:**
- *Gilford Motor Co. v. Horne (1933)* — Used to perpetrate fraud
- *DHN Food Distributors v. Tower Hamlets (1976)* — Group company treated as one
- *State of U.P. v. Renusagar Power Co. (1992)* — Supreme Court allowed veil piercing

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Clauses of Memorandum of Association (MOA):**

The MOA of a company with share capital must contain the following **six mandatory clauses** as per **Section 4(1) of the Companies Act, 2013**:

**1. Name Clause (Clause I):**
The legal name of the company must be stated. The name must end with "Limited" (for public) or "Private Limited" (for private). The name must not be identical to any existing company or violate the Emblems Act.

**2. Registered Office Clause (Clause II):**
The State in which the registered office of the company will be situated must be stated. The exact address is not required at the time of MOA.

**3. Object Clause (Clause III):**
The **objects for which the company is proposed to be incorporated** must be stated. This clause defines the scope of activities and powers of the company. Acts beyond this clause are **ultra vires** and void.

**4. Liability Clause (Clause IV):**
The nature of liability of the members must be stated — whether **limited by shares** or **limited by guarantee** or **unlimited**.

**5. Capital Clause (Clause V):**
For companies with share capital, the amount of authorized share capital must be stated, divided into shares of fixed amounts.

**6. Subscription Clause (Clause VI):**
The signatures of the subscribers, their details (name, address, occupation, number of shares), must be appended. Each subscriber must sign in the presence of one witness.

**Procedure for Alteration of MOA (Section 13):**

The MOA can be altered by the following procedure:

**Step 1:** The **Board of Directors** must pass a resolution recommending the alteration.

**Step 2:** A **Special Resolution** must be passed by the members at a General Meeting (AGM or EGM). At least 75% of votes cast must be in favor.

**Step 3:** For certain changes (e.g., name change, object change, liability change), approval of the **Central Government (ROC)** is required.

**Step 4:** The company must file the altered MOA with the ROC within **15 days** of the resolution.

**Step 5:** The alteration takes effect from the date of resolution, unless specified otherwise.

**Special Cases Requiring Central Government Approval:**
- Change of name involving suffix removal
- Change of objects (Section 13(1))
- Change of registered office from one state to another
- Reduction of share capital (Section 66)

**Limitations on Alteration:**
- The alteration must be within the **bounds of the Companies Act**.
- The alteration must not be **fraudulent or ultra vires the Act**.
- The alteration must not affect the **rights of creditors**.
- A dissenting shareholder may apply to the **Tribunal (NCLT)** if their rights are prejudicially affected.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Mistake 1:** Writing that a company can be incorporated by **any number of people** — Wrong. The minimum is **2 for Private**, **7 for Public**, **1 for OPC**.
> 
> 2. **Mistake 2:** Confusing the **Certificate of Incorporation (COI)** with the **Certificate of Commencement of Business** — They are different. COI is issued at incorporation; CoB is required for public companies under Section 11.
> 
> 3. **Mistake 3:** Writing that the company can **sue on pre-incorporation contracts** — Wrong. The company cannot ratify such contracts (Kelantan Coconut case).
> 
> 4. **Mistake 4:** Confusing **MOA with AOA** — MOA is the **Charter** (objects); AOA is the **Rulebook** (management).
> 
> 5. **Mistake 5:** Not mentioning the **case laws** — Always cite relevant case law for full marks (e.g., Salomon v. Salomon, Kelantan Coconut, Ashbury Railway).
> 
> 6. **Mistake 6:** Writing that the ROC has **discretion to refuse registration** — The certificate of incorporation is **conclusive evidence** under Section 7(2); the ROC cannot deny it.
> 
> 7. **Mistake 7:** Forgetting to mention that **PAN and TAN are auto-generated** with the COI post-2021 amendment.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

✅ **Incorporation** = Legal birth of a company; confers separate legal entity status.

✅ **Minimum Members:** 2 (Private) | 7 (Public) | 1 (OPC).

✅ **Three Core Documents:** MOA (Charter), AOA (Rulebook), INC-9 (Declaration).

✅ **SPICe+ (INC-32)** is the single integrated form for incorporation since 2020.

✅ **Certificate of Incorporation (INC-11)** = Conclusive evidence of registration under Section 7(2).

✅ **DIN** is mandatory for all directors under Section 153.

✅ **MOA has 6 clauses:** Name, Registered Office, Objects, Liability, Capital, Subscription.

✅ **Doctrine of Ultra Vires** applies to MOA; **Doctrine of Constructive Notice** and **Indoor Management** apply to AOA.

✅ **Salomon v. Salomon (1897)** = Foundation case for separate legal entity.

✅ **Kelantan Coconut v. National Union (1955)** = Pre-incorporation contracts are void ab initio.

✅ **PAN and TAN** are auto-generated and printed on the COI post-2021 amendment.

✅ **INC-20A** (Commencement of Business) must be filed within 180 days for share capital companies.

✅ **Limited Liability** is limited to the unpaid amount on shares (Section 3(1)(a)).

✅ **Perpetual Succession** means the company continues regardless of changes in membership.

✅ **Lifting the Corporate Veil** is permitted in exceptional cases of fraud or group company scenarios.

✅ **Doctrine of Indoor Management (Turquand's Case)** — Outsiders can assume internal compliance.

✅ **Doctrine of Constructive Notice** — Deemed knowledge of MOA and AOA.

✅ **Authorized Capital** is the maximum capital a company can issue; **Paid-up Capital** is what has been actually paid.

✅ **ROC** operates under the **Ministry of Corporate Affairs (MCA), Government of India**.

✅ **Form RUN** = Reserve Unique Name (₹1,000 fee per application).

✅ **Form INC-22** = Notice of situation of registered office.

✅ **First Board Meeting** must be held within 30 days of incorporation.

✅ **First AGM** must be held within 9 months from the end of the first financial year.

---

**End of Module 3 Notes — Incorporation and Registration** 📚

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capital
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET834 - BUSINESS ORGANIZATION AND DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Capital

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Capital

In the context of business organization and development, **Capital** refers to the total pool of funds (financial resources) employed by an enterprise to finance its operations, acquire assets, and generate economic value. It represents the wealth or monetary resources contributed by owners or raised through borrowing, which is invested in the business to produce goods and services.

As per standard economic and business management terminology aligned with the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, capital is broadly classified into two principal categories:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Capital (KTU Definition):** Capital is the lifeblood of any business enterprise, encompassing all forms of wealth—whether in **money, machinery, or material form**—that are invested with the expectation of generating future income or profit. It is the foundation upon which the productive capacity of a business is built.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you want to start a small bakery. You cannot bake bread without an oven, flour, butter, or a rented shop. All of these require **money upfront** before you can sell your first loaf and earn revenue. This initial pool of money and resources is your **Capital**.

**Real-World Analogy:**
Think of capital as the **fuel tank of a vehicle**. Without fuel, even the most powerful engine (your business idea) cannot move. The size of the fuel tank (capital base) determines how far the vehicle (your business) can travel before it needs refueling (additional investment or revenue).

| Business Element | Analogy Mapping |
|------------------|-----------------|
| Business Idea | Vehicle Engine |
| Capital | Fuel Tank |
| Revenue | Destination Reached |
| Profit | Extra Fuel Saved |

---

## 1.3 Types of Capital — Overview

Capital can be classified into several fundamental types based on its nature, duration, and purpose:

1. **Fixed Capital** — Invested in long-term assets (land, building, machinery).
2. **Working Capital** — Used for day-to-day operations (raw materials, wages, bills).
3. **Owned Capital (Equity)** — Raised from owners/shareholders.
4. **Borrowed Capital (Debt)** — Raised through loans, debentures, bonds.
5. **Human Capital** — Value derived from employee skills and knowledge.
6. **Intellectual Capital** — Intangible assets like patents, trademarks, brands.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** For Module 3, the primary focus is on understanding the **types of capital**, the **importance of capital in business**, **sources of raising capital**, and **basic capital structure decisions** faced by entrepreneurs and managers.

---

## 1.4 The Role of Capital in Business Development

Capital plays three foundational roles:

- **Productive Role:** Capital enables the acquisition of factors of production (land, labor, machinery).
- **Liquidity Role:** Adequate working capital ensures smooth daily operations.
- **Strategic Role:** Sufficient capital allows for expansion, innovation, and competitive advantage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Concept:** The phrase **"Capital is to business what blood is to the human body"** is a classic management adage emphasizing that without adequate capital flow, no business can survive, grow, or develop.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Classification of Capital

### 2.1.1 Fixed Capital

Fixed capital represents funds invested in **permanent or long-term assets** that are not consumed or converted into cash within the normal accounting cycle (typically **12 months**).

**Characteristics:**
- Long-term in nature (useful life > 1 year).
- Not meant for resale.
- Depreciates over time.
- Examples: Land, Buildings, Plant & Machinery, Furniture, Patents.

### 2.1.2 Working Capital

Working capital refers to the **short-term funds** required to meet day-to-day operational expenses.

**Formula:**

$$WC = CA - CL$$

Where:
- $WC$ = Working Capital
- $CA$ = Current Assets
- $CL$ = Current Liabilities

> [!NOTE]
> **Net Working Capital** is the difference between current assets and current liabilities. **Positive WC** indicates liquidity; **Negative WC** indicates financial distress.

### 2.1.3 Owned vs. Borrowed Capital

| Basis | Owned Capital | Borrowed Capital |
|-------|---------------|------------------|
| Source | Shareholders / Owners | Banks, Financial Institutions, Public |
| Obligation | No fixed repayment | Fixed interest + principal repayment |
| Risk | Lower (no mandatory payment) | Higher (default risk) |
| Cost | Dividend (variable) | Interest (fixed) |
| Control | Dilutes ownership | Does not dilute ownership |

---

## 2.2 Sources of Capital

### A. Short-Term Sources (for Working Capital)

- Trade Credit (supplier credit)
- Bank Overdraft
- Short-term loans
- Bill discounting
- Commercial paper

### B. Long-Term Sources (for Fixed Capital)

- **Equity Capital:** Issue of shares (IPO, Rights Issue, Bonus Shares)
- **Preference Shares:** Hybrid instrument with fixed dividend
- **Debentures / Bonds:** Long-term debt instruments
- **Term Loans:** From banks and financial institutions
- **Retained Earnings:** Profits ploughed back into business
- **Venture Capital / Private Equity:** For startups and high-growth firms

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---------|---------|-------------------|
| Working Capital | $WC = CA - CL$ | $CA$ = Current Assets (INR); $CL$ = Current Liabilities (INR) |
| Current Ratio | $CR = \dfrac{CA}{CL}$ | Liquidity Measure (Ideal $\approx 2:1$) |
| Quick Ratio | $QR = \dfrac{CA - Inventory}{CL}$ | Acid Test Ratio (Ideal $\approx 1:1$) |
| Debt-Equity Ratio | $DER = \dfrac{Total\ Debt}{Total\ Equity}$ | Leverage Measure |
| Return on Capital Employed | $ROCE = \dfrac{EBIT}{Capital\ Employed} \times 100$ | Profitability (%) |
| Capital Employed | $CE = Total\ Assets - Current\ Liabilities$ | Long-term funds (INR) |
| Interest Coverage Ratio | $ICR = \dfrac{EBIT}{Interest\ Expense}$ | Debt Servicing Capacity |
| Cost of Equity (Dividend Model) | $K_e = \dfrac{D_1}{P_0} + g$ | $D_1$ = Expected Dividend; $P_0$ = Market Price; $g$ = Growth Rate |
| Cost of Debt | $K_d = \dfrac{I(1 - t)}{NP}$ | $I$ = Interest; $t$ = Tax Rate; $NP$ = Net Proceeds |
| Weighted Average Cost of Capital | $WACC = (W_e \times K_e) + (W_d \times K_d)$ | $W$ = Weight of Component |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Boundary Conditions:**
> - Current Ratio must always be **> 1** for a healthy firm.
> - DER (Debt-Equity Ratio) ideally should be **< 2:1** in manufacturing; varies by industry.
> - WACC must always be **less than ROCE** for value creation.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Capital Management

Effective capital management ensures:

1. **Operational Continuity** — Avoiding insolvency due to cash crunches.
2. **Optimal Resource Allocation** — Funds deployed in highest-yield projects.
3. **Shareholder Wealth Maximization** — Minimizing WACC and maximizing ROCE.
4. **Strategic Growth** — Funding expansion, R&D, and market entry.
5. **Crisis Resilience** — Buffer against economic downturns.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Application:** In the **startup ecosystem** (e.g., Kerala's emerging tech hubs in Kochi and Trivandrum), venture capital plays a pivotal role. In **traditional industries** (coir, spices, cashew), bank credit and government schemes (Mudra, PMEGP) form the primary capital sources.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Computation of Working Capital

### Given Problem Framework

A manufacturing firm reports the following balance sheet items:

- Current Assets (CA) = ₹ 5,00,000
- Current Liabilities (CL) = ₹ 2,00,000
- Inventory = ₹ 1,50,000

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Calculate Gross Working Capital**

$$GWC = CA = 5,00,000$$

**Step 2: Calculate Net Working Capital**

$$NWC = CA - CL$$

$$NWC = 5,00,000 - 2,00,000 = 3,00,000$$

**Step 3: Calculate Current Ratio**

$$CR = \dfrac{CA}{CL} = \dfrac{5,00,000}{2,00,000} = 2.5$$

**Step 4: Calculate Quick (Acid Test) Ratio**

$$QR = \dfrac{CA - Inventory}{CL}$$

$$QR = \dfrac{5,00,000 - 1,50,000}{2,00,000} = \dfrac{3,50,000}{2,00,000} = 1.75$$

**Step 5: Interpretation**

- Current Ratio = 2.5 → **Healthy** (above ideal 2:1).
- Quick Ratio = 1.75 → **Strong** (above ideal 1:1).
- Net Working Capital is **positive ₹ 3,00,000**, indicating strong short-term liquidity.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)

A firm has the following capital structure:

| Source | Amount (₹) | Cost (%) |
|--------|-------------|----------|
| Equity | 6,00,000 | 15% |
| Debt | 4,00,000 | 10% (after tax) |

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Calculate Total Capital**

$$Total\ Capital = Equity + Debt$$

$$Total\ Capital = 6,00,000 + 4,00,000 = 10,00,000$$

**Step 2: Calculate Weights**

$$W_e = \dfrac{Equity}{Total} = \dfrac{6,00,000}{10,00,000} = 0.60$$

$$W_d = \dfrac{Debt}{Total} = \dfrac{4,00,000}{10,00,000} = 0.40$$

**Step 3: Apply WACC Formula**

$$WACC = (W_e \times K_e) + (W_d \times K_d)$$

$$WACC = (0.60 \times 15) + (0.40 \times 10)$$

$$WACC = 9.0 + 4.0 = 13.0\%$$

**Step 4: Decision Rule**

If the firm's **Return on Capital Employed (ROCE)** is greater than **13%**, the firm creates value; otherwise, it destroys shareholder wealth.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Capital Decision Framework

| Parameter | Equity-Heavy Firm | Debt-Heavy Firm |
|-----------|-------------------|-----------------|
| Financial Risk | Low | High |
| Cost of Capital | Higher (equity is riskier) | Lower (interest is tax-deductible) |
| Control | Diluted | Retained |
| Earnings Volatility | Stable | Amplified |
| Bankruptcy Risk | Low | High |
| Tax Benefit | No | Yes (interest is deductible) |

### Derivation of Tax Shield on Debt

$$\text{Tax Shield} = I \times t$$

Where:
- $I$ = Interest paid
- $t$ = Corporate tax rate

**Example:** If a firm pays ₹ 1,00,000 interest at a 30% tax rate:

$$\text{Tax Shield} = 1,00,000 \times 0.30 = 30,000$$

Effective cost of debt:

$$K_d = \dfrac{I(1-t)}{NP} = \dfrac{1,00,000 \times (1 - 0.30)}{NP} = \dfrac{70,000}{NP}$$

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Capital Structure Decision (EPS Approach)

A firm has two financing options:
- **Option 1:** All Equity (10,000 shares @ ₹ 100)
- **Option 2:** Mix (5,000 shares + ₹ 5,00,000 debt @ 12%)

EBIT (Earnings Before Interest and Tax) = ₹ 2,00,000; Tax Rate = 30%

### Option 1: All Equity

$$\text{EBIT} = 2,00,000$$

$$Interest = 0$$

$$EBT = 2,00,000 - 0 = 2,00,000$$

$$\text{Tax} = 2,00,000 \times 0.30 = 60,000$$

$$\text{EAT} = 2,00,000 - 60,000 = 1,40,000$$

$$EPS_1 = \dfrac{1,40,000}{10,000} = ₹ 14$$

### Option 2: Mixed Capital

$$\text{EBIT} = 2,00,000$$

$$Interest = 5,00,000 \times 0.12 = 60,000$$

$$EBT = 2,00,000 - 60,000 = 1,40,000$$

$$\text{Tax} = 1,40,000 \times 0.30 = 42,000$$

$$\text{EAT} = 1,40,000 - 42,000 = 98,000$$

$$EPS_2 = \dfrac{98,000}{5,000} = ₹ 19.6$$

### Decision

$$\Delta EPS = 19.6 - 14 = +5.6$$

> **Conclusion:** Option 2 (debt financing) yields higher EPS, making it preferable *provided financial risk remains manageable.*

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Sources of Long-Term Capital

| Source | Type | Cost | Risk to Firm | Dilution of Control |
|--------|------|------|--------------|---------------------|
| Equity Shares | Owned | High | Low | Yes |
| Preference Shares | Hybrid | Moderate | Moderate | No |
| Debentures | Debt | Low (after tax) | High (interest obligation) | No |
| Term Loans | Debt | Low–Moderate | High | No |
| Retained Earnings | Owned | Lowest | None | No |
| Venture Capital | Owned | Very High | Low | Yes (significant) |
| Convertible Bonds | Hybrid | Moderate | Moderate | Yes (eventually) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define the term "Capital" in the context of business. Differentiate between Fixed Capital and Working Capital.**

**Model Answer:**

**Capital** refers to the total financial resources (funds) invested in a business enterprise to acquire assets, finance operations, and generate profit.

| Basis | Fixed Capital | Working Capital |
|-------|---------------|-----------------|
| Duration | Long-term (>1 year) | Short-term (<1 year) |
| Purpose | Acquisition of permanent assets | Day-to-day operations |
| Nature | Mostly non-liquid | Highly liquid |
| Examples | Land, Machinery, Patents | Cash, Inventory, Debtors |

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is Debt-Equity Ratio? Why is it an important indicator in capital structure decisions?**

**Model Answer:**

**Debt-Equity Ratio (DER)** is a leverage ratio that measures the proportion of a firm's financing contributed by creditors versus owners.

$$DER = \dfrac{Total\ Debt}{Total\ Equity}$$

**Importance:**
- Indicates **financial risk** and **solvency position**.
- High DER → Higher bankruptcy risk but tax benefits on interest.
- Low DER → Safer but higher cost of capital.
- Helps investors assess the **capital structure** stability of the firm.

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Explain the various sources of raising long-term capital for a business enterprise. Discuss the merits and limitations of equity shares and debentures as sources of finance.**

#### Part (a) — Sources of Long-Term Capital (7 Marks)

The major sources of long-term capital are:

1. **Equity Share Capital** — Funds raised by issuing ordinary shares to the public. Permanent in nature; no obligation to repay.

2. **Preference Share Capital** — Hybrid instrument carrying a fixed dividend with priority over equity in dividend and liquidation.

3. **Debentures / Bonds** — Long-term debt instruments issued to the public, carrying a fixed interest rate and a promise to repay principal on maturity.

4. **Term Loans from Banks / Financial Institutions** — Borrowings from commercial banks, NBFCs, and development banks like SIDBI, IFCI.

5. **Retained Earnings** — Profits not distributed as dividends but reinvested in the business.

6. **Venture Capital and Private Equity** — Investment by specialized funds in high-growth potential firms, especially startups.

7. **Convertible Securities** — Instruments convertible from debt to equity after a specified period.

#### Part (b) — Merits and Limitations of Equity Shares vs. Debentures (7 Marks)

| Aspect | Equity Shares | Debentures |
|--------|---------------|------------|
| **Merit 1** | No mandatory repayment — permanent capital | Tax-deductible interest reduces effective cost |
| **Merit 2** | Enhances creditworthiness and borrowing capacity | Does not dilute ownership/control |
| **Merit 3** | Dividend is discretionary — flexibility in cash flows | Fixed obligation aids financial discipline |
| **Limitation 1** | Dilutes ownership and control of promoters | Mandatory interest — fixed financial burden |
| **Limitation 2** | Costlier than debt (no tax shield on dividends) | Increases financial leverage and bankruptcy risk |
| **Limitation 3** | Issue process is lengthy and regulatory | Restricts future borrowing capacity (charge on assets) |

**Conclusion:** An optimal capital structure balances both equity and debt to minimize WACC while maintaining financial stability.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is Working Capital? Explain the different types of working capital. Compute the Net Working Capital and Current Ratio from the following data and comment on the liquidity position:**

- Current Assets: ₹ 8,00,000
- Current Liabilities: ₹ 3,00,000
- Inventory: ₹ 2,00,000

#### Part (a) — Concept and Types of Working Capital (7 Marks)

**Definition:** Working capital refers to the capital required for financing the short-term obligations and day-to-day operations of a business.

**Types of Working Capital:**

1. **Permanent Working Capital** — Minimum level of current assets that must always be maintained.
2. **Variable / Temporary Working Capital** — Additional current assets required to meet seasonal or cyclical demand.
3. **Seasonal Working Capital** — Required during peak business seasons.
4. **Special Working Capital** — Needed for special circumstances (e.g., launching a new product).

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Net Working Capital**

$$NWC = CA - CL = 8,00,000 - 3,00,000 = ₹ 5,00,000$$

**Step 2: Current Ratio**

$$CR = \dfrac{CA}{CL} = \dfrac{8,00,000}{3,00,000} = 2.67$$

**Step 3: Quick Ratio**

$$QR = \dfrac{CA - Inventory}{CL} = \dfrac{8,00,000 - 2,00,000}{3,00,000} = \dfrac{6,00,000}{3,00,000} = 2.0$$

**Step 4: Liquidity Comment**

- Current Ratio of **2.67** is above the ideal benchmark of **2:1** → strong short-term solvency.
- Quick Ratio of **2.0** is well above the ideal benchmark of **1:1** → strong immediate liquidity without inventory dependence.
- Net Working Capital of **₹ 5,00,000** is positive and substantial → the firm has a healthy operational cushion.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often forget to **subtract inventory** when calculating Quick Ratio — full marks lost.
> - Always state the **ideal benchmark** (2:1 for CR, 1:1 for QR) before commenting on liquidity.
> - Marks are awarded for **interpretation**, not just numerical computation. Always add a concluding remark.
> - Do **not** confuse **Gross Working Capital** (CA) with **Net Working Capital** (CA - CL).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Capital** = total funds (equity + debt) used to finance business assets and operations.
- **Fixed Capital** is invested in long-term, non-liquid assets; **Working Capital** supports daily operations.
- **Net Working Capital** = $CA - CL$; must be **positive** for liquidity.
- **Current Ratio** ideal = **2:1**; **Quick Ratio** ideal = **1:1**.
- **Sources of Capital** are categorized into **Owned (Equity, Retained Earnings, VC)** and **Borrowed (Debt, Debentures, Loans)**.
- **Cost of Equity > Cost of Debt**, but debt provides **tax shield** through interest deductibility.
- **WACC** = Weighted average of cost of equity and cost of debt; firm must aim for **ROCE > WACC**.
- **Debt-Equity Ratio** indicates financial leverage; optimal varies by industry (typically < 2:1).
- **Capital Structure** decisions impact both **EPS** and **financial risk** — trade-off analysis is essential.
- For KTU exams, always remember to: **state formula → substitute values → interpret result**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**
> - [x] Define Capital (3 marks)
> - [x] Differentiate Fixed vs Working Capital (3 marks)
> - [x] List Sources of Long-term and Short-term Capital (7 marks)
> - [x] Discuss Merits of Equity vs Debentures (7 marks)
> - [x] Compute Working Capital Ratios (7 marks)
> - [x] Comment on Liquidity Position (interpretation marks)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sources of Capital
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET834 - BUSINESS ORGANIZATION AND DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sources of Capital

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sources of Capital** refer to the various channels, instruments, and financial avenues through which a business enterprise raises the monetary funds (working capital and fixed capital) required to establish, operate, expand, and sustain its commercial activities. In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme, the term encompasses both **Owned Funds (Owner's Equity)** and **Borrowed Funds (Debt Capital)**, classified under short-term, medium-term, and long-term financing horizons.

In formal corporate finance terminology, capital represents the **total pool of money** available to a firm for productive deployment. The capital structure of a business is the specific combination of debt and equity that a company uses to finance its overall operations and growth. Sources of capital are essentially the **origins** from which this financial fuel is tapped.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Blood Circulation" Analogy of Business Capital**

Imagine a business as a **living human body**.
- **Capital** is the **blood** that flows through the body (the company).
- **Sources of capital** are the various **organs and veins** that produce, purify, and pump this blood.

Just like a human body needs:
- **Oxygen-rich blood from the heart** (Owned Capital / Equity) — the lifeblood generated internally
- **External food intake for nutrients** (Borrowed Capital / Debt) — supplemental fuel acquired from outside
- **Vitamin supplements for specific deficiencies** (Venture Capital, Angel Funds) — specialized injections for rapid growth
- **Blood transfusions in emergencies** (Bank Overdrafts, Trade Credit) — short-term survival mechanisms

A healthy business needs the **right mix** of all these sources. Too much owned capital (equity) dilutes ownership; too much debt creates a **debt trap** and increases the **Debt-Equity Ratio (D/E)**. The art of financial management is choosing the **right proportions** of these sources — much like a cardiologist balancing a patient's blood parameters.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> Under the 2024 NEP-aligned scheme, students must be able to:
> 1. Identify and classify at least **8 distinct sources of capital**
> 2. Distinguish between **owned** and **borrowed** capital
> 3. Evaluate the **cost, risk, and control implications** of each source
> 4. Recommend the **appropriate source** based on business stage (startup, growth, maturity)

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

The following **bold metrics** form the analytical backbone of this topic:

- **Cost of Capital (CoC):** The effective interest rate a firm pays for its financing (debt + equity combined).
- **Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC):** The blended cost of all capital sources weighted by their proportion in the capital structure.
- **Debt-Equity Ratio (D/E):** A leverage metric showing the relative use of borrowed funds to owned funds.
- **Venture Capital Burn Rate:** The rate at which a startup spends its raised venture capital before generating positive cash flow.
- **Leverage:** The use of borrowed funds to amplify the potential return on equity.
- **Dilution of Control:** The reduction in ownership percentage when new shares are issued.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Two-Pillar Classification of Capital

Every rupee that enters a business originates from one of **two fundamental pillars**:

#### **Pillar A: Owned Capital (Owner's Funds / Equity Capital)**

This represents the **owner's own stake** in the business. The business is **not legally obligated** to repay this money, and the owners become **residual claimants** — they get paid only after all debts are settled.

**Sub-categories of Owned Capital:**

- **i. Equity Share Capital:** Raised by issuing **ordinary shares** to the public. Shareholders become part-owners, enjoy voting rights, and receive dividends (which are not mandatory).
- **ii. Preference Share Capital:** A hybrid instrument carrying a **fixed dividend** (paid before equity shareholders) and typically **no voting rights**.
- **iii. Retained Earnings (Internal Accruals):** Profits that are **ploughed back** into the business instead of being distributed as dividends. This is the cheapest source of capital as there is **no flotation cost**.
- **iv. Capital Introduction by Promoters:** The initial capital invested by the founders of the company, also called **promoter's contribution** or **seed capital**.
- **v. Venture Capital (VC) & Private Equity (PE):** Funds raised from specialized investors in exchange for equity, usually at high-growth stages.
- **vi. Angel Investors:** Wealthy individuals who invest their personal funds in early-stage startups in return for equity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Owned Capital Matters:** It establishes **credibility, creditworthiness, and solvency** in the eyes of lenders. A business with no own contribution is rarely trusted by banks.

#### **Pillar B: Borrowed Capital (Debt Capital / Loan Capital)**

This represents **funds borrowed from external parties** with a **legal obligation to repay** the principal along with interest within a stipulated time frame.

**Sub-categories of Borrowed Capital:**

**B.1 Long-Term Sources (Repayment Period > 5 years):**
- **i. Debentures:** Bonds issued by the company to the public, carrying a fixed interest rate and a fixed maturity date. Types include **secured, unsecured, convertible, and non-convertible debentures**.
- **ii. Long-Term Bank Loans:** Term loans from commercial banks, financial institutions, and NBFCs (e.g., SIDBI, IFCI).
- **iii. Public Deposits:** Invitations to the general public to deposit money with the company, typically for 1–3 years.
- **iv. Foreign Capital / FDI:** Direct investment by foreign companies, often accompanied by technology transfer.

**B.2 Medium-Term Sources (Repayment Period 1–5 years):**
- **i. Lease Financing:** Acquiring assets on rent where the lessee pays periodic lease rentals and may acquire ownership at the end.
- **ii. Hire-Purchase:** Acquiring assets by paying in installments where ownership transfers only after the final payment.
- **iii. Deferred Credit / Instalment Credit:** Credit from suppliers for purchase of machinery, payable in installments.

**B.3 Short-Term Sources (Repayment Period < 1 year):**
- **i. Bank Overdraft:** Withdrawing more money than deposited in a current account, up to a sanctioned limit.
- **ii. Cash Credit (CC):** A flexible loan facility where the borrower can withdraw up to a limit and repay as cash flows permit.
- **iii. Trade Credit:** Credit extended by suppliers allowing the buyer to pay after delivery (e.g., "Net 30" terms).
- **iv. Bill of Exchange / Promissory Notes:** Short-term negotiable instruments used to settle business transactions.
- **v. Factoring:** Selling accounts receivable to a third party (factor) at a discount for immediate cash.
- **vi. Commercial Paper (CP):** Unsecured short-term promissory notes issued by large corporations in the money market.
- **vii. Customer Advances:** Money received in advance from customers before delivery of goods/services.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is a high-yield revision asset — memorize the formulas and their applications.**

| **Formula / Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Meaning & Units** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Debt-Equity Ratio** | $D/E = \dfrac{\text{Total Debt}}{\text{Total Equity}}$ | A leverage ratio; no unit, expressed as a decimal or ratio | Measures financial risk. KTU benchmark: ideally $\leq 2:1$ for manufacturing firms |
| **Proprietary Ratio** | $PR = \dfrac{\text{Shareholders' Funds}}{\text{Total Assets}}$ | A solvency ratio; no unit | Measures owner's contribution to total assets. Higher = more stable |
| **WACC (Weighted Average Cost of Capital)** | $WACC = \left( \dfrac{E}{V} \times R_e \right) + \left( \dfrac{D}{V} \times R_d \times (1 - T) \right)$ | $E$=Equity, $D$=Debt, $V$=Total capital, $R_e$=Cost of equity, $R_d$=Cost of debt, $T$=Tax rate | Blended cost of financing; used in capital budgeting decisions |
| **Cost of Equity (Dividend Model)** | $R_e = \dfrac{D_1}{P_0} + g$ | $D_1$=Expected dividend, $P_0$=Current share price, $g$=Growth rate | Minimum return equity investors expect |
| **Cost of Debt (After-Tax)** | $R_d (\text{after tax}) = R_d \times (1 - T)$ | Interest rate adjusted for tax shield benefit | Effective cost of borrowing considering tax deductibility |
| **Interest Coverage Ratio** | $ICR = \dfrac{EBIT}{\text{Interest Expense}}$ | $EBIT$=Earnings Before Interest & Tax | A ratio > 3 is considered safe; below 1.5 signals distress |
| **Venture Capital Dilution** | $\text{New Ownership \%} = \dfrac{\text{Funds Raised}}{\text{Post-Money Valuation}}$ | Post-money valuation = Pre-money valuation + New investment | Determines how much equity the founder must give up |
| **EPS (Earnings Per Share)** | $EPS = \dfrac{\text{Net Profit after Tax}}{\text{Total Number of Equity Shares}}$ | Profitability per share (in ₹) | Indicator of share attractiveness |

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Business Fields

- **Startup Founders** use the equity-debt framework to decide whether to bootstrap (owned capital) or seek VC funding.
- **Mechanical/Civil Engineers** running construction firms rely heavily on **term loans and equipment leasing** for purchasing machinery.
- **IT Engineers** founding tech startups leverage **angel funding and venture capital** to scale products before revenue generation.
- **Financial Analysts** in any industry use **D/E and WACC** to evaluate the capital structure health of competitor firms.
- **Production Managers** use **working capital sources** (overdraft, trade credit) to fund day-to-day operations smoothly.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of WACC — A KTU Favourite

The **Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)** is the most frequently tested formula in this module. Let us derive it step by step.

**Step 1: Define the Total Capital Structure Value**

Let $V$ be the total market value of the firm's capital. This consists of two components:
- $E$ = Market value of equity
- $D$ = Market value of debt

$$V = E + D$$

**Step 2: Express the Weight of Each Component**

The weight of equity in the capital structure is $w_E = \dfrac{E}{V}$

The weight of debt in the capital structure is $w_D = \dfrac{D}{V}$

These weights must satisfy the identity: $w_E + w_D = 1$

**Step 3: Identify the Cost of Each Component**

- Cost of equity: $R_e$ (required rate of return by shareholders)
- Cost of debt (before tax): $R_d$ (interest rate paid on debt)
- Tax rate applicable to the firm: $T$

**Step 4: Apply the Tax Shield to Debt**

Interest on debt is a **tax-deductible expense**, so the effective cost of debt is reduced. The after-tax cost of debt is:

$$R_{d,\text{after tax}} = R_d \times (1 - T)$$

**Step 5: Compute the Weighted Average**

Multiply each component's cost by its weight and sum:

$$\begin{aligned}
WACC &= w_E \cdot R_e + w_D \cdot R_{d,\text{after tax}} \\
&= \frac{E}{V} \cdot R_e + \frac{D}{V} \cdot R_d \cdot (1 - T)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Verify Using a Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)**

Suppose a firm has:
- Equity = ₹60 lakh, $R_e$ = 14%
- Debt = ₹40 lakh, $R_d$ = 10%, Tax rate = 30%

$$\begin{aligned}
V &= E + D = 60 + 40 = 100 \text{ lakh} \\
w_E &= \frac{60}{100} = 0.6 \\
w_D &= \frac{40}{100} = 0.4 \\
R_{d,\text{after tax}} &= 0.10 \times (1 - 0.30) = 0.07 = 7\% \\
WACC &= (0.6 \times 0.14) + (0.4 \times 0.07) \\
&= 0.084 + 0.028 \\
&= 0.112 = 11.2\%
\end{aligned}$$

The firm's WACC is **11.2%**. Any project yielding less than this return will destroy shareholder value.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Debt-Equity Ratio and Its Risk Interpretation

**Step 1: Define the Numerator and Denominator**

$$D/E = \frac{\text{Long-Term Borrowings + Short-Term Borrowings}}{\text{Shareholders' Funds (Equity + Reserves)}}$$

**Step 2: Interpret the Numerical Value**

- $D/E < 1$: Conservatively financed. Low risk, low return on equity.
- $D/E = 1$: Balanced capital structure. Moderate risk.
- $D/E > 2$: Aggressively financed. High financial risk and interest burden.

**Step 3: Numerical Illustration**

A company has the following balance sheet items:
- Equity Share Capital: ₹50 lakh
- Reserves & Surplus: ₹20 lakh
- 12% Debentures: ₹60 lakh
- Bank Loan: ₹30 lakh

$$\begin{aligned}
D &= 60 + 30 = 90 \text{ lakh} \\
E &= 50 + 20 = 70 \text{ lakh} \\
D/E &= \frac{90}{70} = 1.29
\end{aligned}$$

This indicates a **moderately leveraged** capital structure.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Source of Capital

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following matrix is a high-yield KTU tool — businesses choose sources based on 5 key parameters.**

| **Parameter** | **Equity Shares** | **Preference Shares** | **Debentures** | **Bank Loan** | **Venture Capital** | **Trade Credit** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cost of Capital** | High (15–20%) | Medium (10–14%) | Low-Medium (8–12%) | Medium (10–13%) | Very High (25%+) | Free (interest-free) |
| **Repayment Obligation** | No (perpetual) | No (perpetual) | Yes (fixed maturity) | Yes (instalments) | No (exit-based) | Yes (short term) |
| **Control Dilution** | Yes (voting rights) | Minimal (no voting) | No | No | Yes (board seat) | No |
| **Risk to Firm** | Low (no fixed payment) | Medium (fixed dividend) | High (fixed interest) | High (fixed EMI) | Low (no fixed cost) | Low |
| **Tax Deductibility of Cost** | No (dividend not deductible) | No | Yes (interest deductible) | Yes | No | N/A |
| **Suitable Business Stage** | Growth & Maturity | Stable mature firms | Established firms | All stages | Early-stage startups | Operating firms |
| **Flotation Cost** | High | Medium | Medium-High | Low | Very High | Negligible |
| **Flexibility** | Low | Low | Low | Medium | High | Very High |

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Venture Capital Dilution Calculation (KTU Hot Topic)

**Scenario:** A startup has a **pre-money valuation** of ₹9 crore. A VC firm invests ₹3 crore. Calculate the founder's post-investment ownership.

**Step 1: Compute Post-Money Valuation**

$$V_{\text{post}} = V_{\text{pre}} + \text{Investment} = 9 + 3 = 12 \text{ crore}$$

**Step 2: Compute VC's Ownership Percentage**

$$\text{VC Ownership \%} = \frac{\text{Investment}}{V_{\text{post}}} = \frac{3}{12} = 0.25 = 25\%$$

**Step 3: Compute Founder's Diluted Ownership**

$$\text{Founder \%} = 1 - 0.25 = 0.75 = 75\%$$

The founder previously owned 100%; after the VC infusion, ownership drops to **75%** — this is the **dilution effect**.

### 3.5 Time-Value-Based Cost Comparison: Retained Earnings vs. New Equity

**Why Retained Earnings is the "Cheapest" Source:**

Although there is **no explicit flotation cost**, retained earnings have an **opportunity cost** — the foregone dividends that shareholders could have received.

$$\text{Cost of Retained Earnings} = R_e = \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g$$

Whereas **cost of fresh equity** has an additional **flotation cost ($F$)** percentage:

$$R_{e,\text{new}} = \frac{D_1}{P_0 (1 - F)} + g$$

Since $(1 - F) < 1$, it follows that $R_{e,\text{new}} > R_{e,\text{retained}}$. Therefore, **retained earnings are cheaper** than fresh equity issues.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q1. Differentiate between Owned Capital and Borrowed Capital.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Basis** | **Owned Capital** | **Borrowed Capital** |
|---|---|---|
| **Source** | Contributed by owners/shareholders | Obtained from external lenders |
| **Obligation to Repay** | Not legally required to repay principal | Must be repaid within a fixed period |
| **Cost** | Dividends (not tax-deductible) | Interest (tax-deductible) |
| **Risk to Firm** | Low (no fixed payment obligation) | High (fixed EMI/interest burden) |
| **Control** | Voting rights dilute ownership | Lenders usually have no voting rights |
| **Examples** | Equity shares, retained earnings, VC | Debentures, bank loans, trade credit |

> **(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 1.5 marks for 3–4 valid distinctions + 0.5 mark for examples)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. What is Venture Capital? Mention two of its key features.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Venture Capital is a form of private equity financing provided by specialized investment firms to **early-stage, high-potential, high-risk startups** in exchange for equity ownership, with the VC firm expecting an **exit route** (IPO or acquisition) within 5–7 years.

**Key Features:**
1. **Equity Participation:** VCs take a share in the ownership, not just interest payments.
2. **Long-Term Horizon:** Investment is held for 5–7 years before exit.
3. **High Risk–High Return:** Investments are risky, but successful exits can yield 10x–100x returns.
4. **Beyond Money:** VCs also provide **mentorship, network access, and strategic guidance**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3 Internal Choice]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):**

> **"Capital is the lifeblood of a business." In the light of this statement, explain the various sources of capital available to a business enterprise. Classify them into owned and borrowed sources, and discuss the factors affecting the choice of capital structure.**

**Sub-Part (a) — Classification of Capital Sources (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Capital can be classified into two broad categories: **Owned Capital** and **Borrowed Capital**.

**1. Owned Capital (Equity Funds):**
- *Equity Share Capital:* Raised by issuing ordinary shares. Shareholders are part-owners with voting rights and variable dividends.
- *Preference Share Capital:* Carries a fixed dividend, priority over equity in dividend and repayment, but usually no voting rights.
- *Retained Earnings:* Profits not distributed as dividends but reinvested in the business. It is the cheapest source as it carries no flotation cost.
- *Venture Capital & Angel Funds:* Equity funding from specialized investors for high-growth startups in exchange for ownership stakes.
- *Promoter's Contribution:* The initial capital brought in by the company's founders, demonstrating their commitment.

**2. Borrowed Capital (Debt Funds):**
- *Long-Term Sources:* Debentures (secured, unsecured, convertible), long-term bank loans, public deposits, and Foreign Direct Investment (FDI).
- *Medium-Term Sources:* Lease financing, hire-purchase, and deferred credit from suppliers.
- *Short-Term Sources:* Bank overdraft, cash credit, trade credit, factoring, commercial paper, and customer advances.

**3. Hybrid Instruments:**
- *Convertible Debentures:* Debt that can be converted into equity shares at a future date, blending debt and equity features.

**Diagrammatic Classification (suggested in exam):**

```
                Capital Sources
        ┌──────────────┴──────────────┐
   Owned Capital              Borrowed Capital
   ┌────┴────┐                 ┌─────┼─────┐
Equity  Pref.Shares         Long   Medium  Short
```

**(7 marks: 1 mark for overview + 3 marks for owned capital sub-categories + 2 marks for borrowed capital sub-categories + 1 mark for hybrid mention)**

**Sub-Part (b) — Factors Affecting Choice of Capital Structure (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The capital structure decision (debt vs. equity mix) is influenced by the following factors:

1. **Cost of Capital:** Firms prefer cheaper sources. Debt is cheaper due to the tax shield, but excessive debt increases financial risk.

2. **Risk Considerations:**
   - *Financial Risk:* Higher debt = higher fixed interest obligations = higher bankruptcy risk.
   - *Business Risk:* Firms in volatile industries (e.g., technology, fashion) should use less debt.

3. **Tax Shield Benefit:** Interest on debt is tax-deductible, lowering the effective cost. Firms in higher tax brackets benefit more from debt.

4. **Control and Ownership:** If existing owners wish to retain full control, they prefer debt over equity (which dilutes ownership).

5. **Floatation Costs:** Issuing new shares involves high legal and underwriting costs, while bank loans are cheaper to arrange.

6. **Flexibility:** Debt imposes strict covenants and repayment schedules; equity provides more operational flexibility.

7. **Market Conditions:** When share prices are overvalued, firms prefer equity; when interest rates are low, debt is favoured.

8. **Nature of Assets:** Firms with substantial fixed assets (real estate, manufacturing) can use debt more confidently as collateral.

9. **Profitability and Cash Flow Stability:** Profitable firms with stable cash flows can comfortably service higher debt.

10. **Legal and Regulatory Constraints:** Certain industries (e.g., banking) face restrictions on leverage ratios.

**(7 marks: 1.5 marks each for 4–5 well-explained factors, with at least 1 numerical or situational example)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

> **Explain the concept of Venture Capital as a source of finance. Discuss its features, stages of financing, and the differences between Venture Capital and traditional bank loans.**

**Sub-Part (a) — Concept, Features, and Stages of Venture Capital (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept:**
Venture Capital (VC) is a form of private equity investment provided to **early-stage, high-growth potential startups** that are too risky for traditional bank financing. VCs take equity stakes and expect substantial returns through an **exit event** such as an IPO or acquisition.

**Distinctive Features of Venture Capital:**

1. **Equity Financing:** Unlike loans, VCs take ownership shares, not debt instruments.
2. **Long Investment Horizon:** Typically 5–7 years before exit.
3. **High Risk–High Reward Profile:** Most VC investments fail, but the few successes deliver outsized returns.
4. **Active Involvement:** VCs contribute not only capital but also **mentorship, network, and strategic guidance**.
5. **Stage-Wise Investment:** Capital is released in tranches tied to performance milestones.
6. **Exit Orientation:** Profit is realized through IPO, strategic acquisition, or buyback.

**Stages of Venture Capital Financing:**

| **Stage** | **Description** | **Typical Investment Size** |
|---|---|---|
| **Seed Stage** | Idea validation, prototype development | ₹10 lakh – ₹1 crore |
| **Early Stage (Series A)** | Product launch, initial market entry | ₹1–10 crore |
| **Expansion Stage (Series B, C)** | Scaling operations, market capture | ₹10–100 crore |
| **Mezzanine Stage** | Pre-IPO financing, profitability focus | ₹50+ crore |
| **Exit Stage** | IPO or acquisition to realize returns | Realization event |

**(7 marks: 2 marks for definition + 2 marks for features + 3 marks for stages with example/table)**

**Sub-Part (b) — VC vs. Traditional Bank Loans — Comparative Analysis (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Venture Capital** | **Traditional Bank Loan** |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature of Financing** | Equity (ownership stake) | Debt (loan with interest) |
| **Risk Appetite** | Very high (accepts 70% failure rate) | Low (requires collateral & track record) |
| **Return Expectation** | 10x to 100x via equity appreciation | Fixed interest (10–14% p.a.) |
| **Collateral Requirement** | None (invests in potential) | Mandatory (asset or guarantee) |
| **Repayment Obligation** | None; exit through IPO/acquisition | Strict EMI schedule |
| **Involvement in Business** | Active (board seat, mentorship) | Passive (monitoring only) |
| **Suitable For** | Startups with no profits/collateral | Established profitable businesses |
| **Decision Speed** | Faster (3–6 months) | Moderate (1–3 months) |
| **Cost of Capital** | Very high (effectively 25%+) | Lower (8–14%) |
| **Control Impact** | Dilutes founder's ownership | No ownership dilution |

**Conclusion:** VC is ideal for **innovation-driven, asset-light startups** (e.g., tech firms), while bank loans are better for **asset-heavy, cash-flow-positive businesses** (e.g., manufacturing).

**(7 marks: 1.5 marks for each well-explained difference across 4–5 parameters + 0.5 mark each for examples and conclusion)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Mere listing without classification = 0 marks.** Students often list 10 sources but fail to categorize them as owned/borrowed or short/long-term. Always present a **structured classification**.
> 2. **Ignoring numerical examples:** KTU 2024 scheme rewards **application-level thinking**. Always support your answer with a numerical example (e.g., a WACC calculation, D/E computation, or VC dilution example).
> 3. **Conflating "Venture Capital" and "Angel Investment":** Angel investors use **personal funds** at the **seed stage**; VCs use **pooled institutional funds** at **growth stages**. Mixing them up costs marks.
> 4. **Forgetting the "Tax Shield" concept:** When discussing cost of debt, never omit the $(1 - T)$ factor. This is a **favourite KTU trap question**.
> 5. **Skipping hybrid instruments:** Convertible debentures, preference shares, and mezzanine financing are **frequently asked** and are often ignored by students.
> 6. **Handwriting neatness in flowcharts:** The KTU board often awards 1–2 extra marks for a well-drawn classification tree.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ **Capital = Owned + Borrowed.** This is the foundational binary classification.
- ✅ **Owned capital is cheaper in the long run but dilutes control; borrowed capital is risky but tax-efficient.**
- ✅ **The "4 Most Tested Sources" in KTU exams:** Equity Shares, Debentures, Bank Loans, Venture Capital.
- ✅ **WACC Formula:** $WACC = \left( \dfrac{E}{V} \times R_e \right) + \left( \dfrac{D}{V} \times R_d \times (1 - T) \right)$ — **memorize verbatim**.
- ✅ **D/E Ratio:** Below 1 = conservative, Above 2 = risky. A ratio of **1.5:1** is the textbook benchmark.
- ✅ **Venture Capital = Equity + Mentorship + Exit Orientation.** Not the same as bank loans.
- ✅ **Trade Credit is the cheapest short-term source** — it's interest-free but affects supplier goodwill.
- ✅ **Retained Earnings have an opportunity cost** (foregone dividends), but are still cheaper than fresh equity.
- ✅ **Convertible Debentures** are **hybrid** instruments — debt today, equity tomorrow. Mention them whenever discussing capital structure flexibility.
- ✅ **Lease vs. Hire-Purchase:** Lease = rental, no ownership transfer. Hire-Purchase = instalments with ownership transfer at the end.
- ✅ **For startups:** Promoter Capital → Angel Funding → VC → Bank Loan → IPO (in this natural progression).
- ✅ **For mature firms:** Retained Earnings + Debentures + Public Deposits dominate the capital structure.
- ✅ **Cost hierarchy (lowest to highest):** Trade Credit → Retained Earnings → Bank Loan → Debentures → Preference Shares → Equity Shares → Venture Capital.
- ✅ **The Tax Shield is a key advantage of debt** — interest is deductible; dividends are not.
- ✅ **Always use the analytical matrix** in your answer: Cost | Risk | Control | Flexibility | Tax Benefit.
- ✅ **Stage of business lifecycle determines source choice** — this is a frequently asked 5–7 mark question.
- ✅ **Bonus Tip:** When asked to "recommend" a source, justify using 3–4 factors (cost, risk, control, flexibility) — not just one.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Sources of Capital**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Commencement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET834 - BUSINESS ORGANIZATION AND DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Commencement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Commencement of Business** is the stage at which a legally incorporated company begins its actual business operations, enters into legally binding contracts, raises capital from the public, and exercises its statutory borrowing powers. Under the **Companies Act, 2013**, Section 11 governs the rules for commencement, distinguishing between *Private Companies* and *Public Companies*.

In precise KTU terminology:
- **Incorporation** = The legal birth of a company (issue of Certificate of Incorporation by the Registrar of Companies – ROC).
- **Commencement** = The legal commencement of business operations (issue of Certificate of Commencement of Business).

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of it like the difference between **registering a child's birth** and **the child starting school**:
- **Incorporation** is like getting the **birth certificate** from the municipal authority — it proves the entity *legally exists*.
- **Commencement** is like the child **actually joining school and attending classes** — the entity *starts functioning*.

A company may be legally "born" (incorporated) but cannot legally start working (commence) until the required statutory declarations and certificates are in place.

### 1.3 KTU Syllabus Highlight

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight (Module 3):**
> The topic "Commencement" is typically assessed for **Part A (3 marks)** as a short-answer question and **Part B (14 marks)** as a descriptive question comparing Public vs. Private companies, statutory requirements, and the consequences of non-compliance.

### 1.4 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **ROC** | Registrar of Companies |
| **MCA** | Ministry of Corporate Affairs |
| **MOA** | Memorandum of Association |
| **AOA** | Articles of Association |
| **DIR-12** | Form for appointment of directors |
| **INC-21** | Application for Commencement of Business |
| **Section 10** | Section dealing with effect of incorporation |
| **Section 11** | Section dealing with commencement rules |
| **Section 149(7)** | Public company must have ≥7 members |
| **Section 2(71)** | Definition of Public Company |
| **Section 2(68)** | Definition of Private Company |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The 'Why' Behind the Concept

A company is a **separate legal entity** (Salomon v. Salomon & Co. Ltd., 1897). Because it enjoys limited liability and the privilege of limited liability, the law imposes strict safeguards before it can start dealing with the public, raising deposits, or borrowing money. **Commencement rules exist to protect creditors, shareholders, and the public** from fraudulent or premature business activities.

### 2.2 Logical Breakdown – Statutory Position

#### A. For a Private Company
- Under **Section 11(1)** of the Companies Act, 2013, a **private company** (other than a Section 8 company, deemed public, or NBFC) **can commence its business immediately after incorporation**.
- It does NOT require a Certificate of Commencement of Business.
- It does NOT need to file Form INC-21.

#### B. For a Public Company
A public company must obtain the **Certificate of Commencement of Business** before it can:
1. Exercise borrowing powers.
2. Raise deposits from the public.
3. Enter into legally binding contracts in the course of business.

#### C. Mandatory Pre-Conditions for Public Company Commencement
The company must fulfill the following before applying:

1. **Shares Allotted** – Every share must be allotted (cash consideration) up to the amount stated in the prospectus, and any premium must be either received in cash or capitalized.
2. **Director Declaration** – Every director verifies in Form **DIR-8** that he is not disqualified under Section 164.
3. **No Default in Deposits** – No money is liable to be repaid as deposit.
4. **No Invitation to Public** – No invitation has been made to the public to subscribe to shares/debentures.
5. **ROC Verification** – Filed declaration with the Registrar verifying all the above.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Provision | Private Company | Public Company |
|---|---|---|
| Commencement Time | Immediately after Incorporation | After Certificate of Commencement |
| Section Applicable | Section 11(1) proviso | Section 11(1) |
| Form Required | None | INC-21 |
| Certificate Needed | No | Yes |
| Minimum Members | **2** | **7** |
| Minimum Directors | **2** | **3** |
| Maximum Members | **200** | **Unlimited** |
| Public Subscription | Not Allowed | Allowed |
| Borrowings Allowed | Immediately | Only after commencement |
| Restriction on Invitation | Yes (cannot invite public) | No (after commencement) |
| Time Limit | — | Within **180 days** of incorporation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Statutory Timeframe:** A public company must obtain the Certificate of Commencement within **180 days** from the date of its incorporation. This is a non-extendable period under Section 11(5).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **engineering and technology industry** (which is the core KTU context), the concept of commencement is highly relevant when:
- An **engineering startup** registers as a **Private Limited Company** (quicker — can start operations immediately).
- A **manufacturing firm** registers as a **Public Limited Company** to raise capital from the public, IPO, or FPO.
- **Joint ventures** between foreign companies and Indian partners structure their share allotments before commencement.
- **Section 8 companies** (non-profit) like engineering colleges' R&D wings follow modified rules.

### 2.5 Penalty for Non-Compliance

> [!WARNING]
> **Penalties under Section 11(5):**
> If a public company commences business before obtaining the Certificate of Commencement of Business:
> - The company shall be **liable to a fine of ₹5,000 for every day** of default.
> - Every **officer in default** shall be liable to a fine of **₹1,000 for every day** of default (minimum ₹1 lakh).
> - Contracts entered into before commencement may be declared **void** at the option of the company.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Procedure & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Procedural Flow for Public Company Commencement

This is the **exhaustive step-by-step process** a public company must follow before commencing business. Each step has a specific legal anchor.

$$
\textbf{Step 1: Reservation of Name} \longrightarrow \textbf{Step 2: Filing of MOA \& AOA} \longrightarrow \textbf{Step 3: Issue of Certificate of Incorporation}
$$

$$
\Downarrow
$$

$$
\textbf{Step 4: Allotment of Shares (Cash Consideration)} \longrightarrow \textbf{Step 5: Verification of Director Disqualification (DIR-8)}
$$

$$
\Downarrow
$$

$$
\textbf{Step 6: Verification of No Default on Deposits} \longrightarrow \textbf{Step 7: Verification of No Public Invitation}
$$

$$
\Downarrow
$$

$$
\textbf{Step 8: Filing of Declaration in Form INC-21 with ROC} \longrightarrow \textbf{Step 9: Issue of Certificate of Commencement of Business}
$$

$$
\Downarrow
$$

$$
\textbf{Step 10: Company Begins Business Operations Legally}
$$

### 3.2 Detailed Analysis of Each Step

**Step 1 — Reservation of Name:**
The promoters apply for name availability through Form **RUN (Reserve Unique Name)** on the MCA portal. The name must not be identical to an existing company and must comply with the **Companies (Incorporation Rules), 2014**. The reserved name is valid for **20 days** (extendable by 60 days on payment).

**Step 2 — Filing of MOA and AOA:**
The Memorandum of Association (MOA) defines the company's objects, capital structure, and relationship with the outside world. The Articles of Association (AOA) governs internal management. Both are filed with Form **SPICe+ (Simplified Proforma for Incorporating Company electronically)** along with **AGILE-PRO** for GST, EPFO, ESIC registration.

**Step 3 — Certificate of Incorporation:**
The Registrar of Companies (ROC) scrutinizes the application. If satisfied, a **Certificate of Incorporation** is issued under Section 7(2). This marks the **legal birth** of the company. From this date, the company becomes a **juristic person** capable of entering into contracts.

**Step 4 — Allotment of Shares:**
Under Section 39, a public company must allot shares within **60 days** of receiving the application money. The subscription amount must be at least the amount stated in the prospectus. If not allotted within 60 days, the company must refund the money within **75 days** with interest @ 12% p.a.

**Step 5 — Director Disqualification Verification (DIR-8):**
Every director must file a self-declaration in **Form DIR-8** confirming they are not disqualified under Section 164 (e.g., unsound mind, undischarged insolvent, convicted of offence).

**Step 6 — No Default on Deposits:**
The directors must verify that no deposit remains unpaid or unclaimed beyond the statutory period.

**Step 7 — No Public Invitation:**
Verify that no offer has been made to the public before the company is ready to commence.

**Step 8 — Filing Form INC-21:**
The company files Form **INC-21 (Declaration for Commencement of Business)** with the ROC. The declaration must be signed by:
- A **director** of the company.
- A **chartered accountant** (CA) or **company secretary (CS)** certifying compliance.

**Step 9 — Certificate of Commencement of Business:**
The ROC issues the certificate upon satisfaction. The certificate is conclusive evidence that the company is entitled to commence business.

**Step 10 — Legal Operations Begin:**
The company can now legally:
- Borrow money (issue debentures, secure loans).
- Enter into binding contracts.
- Issue shares to the public.
- Operate bank accounts in the company's name.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix – Commencement vs. Incorporation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Incorporation} &= \text{Legal birth of the company (existence)} \\
\text{Commencement} &= \text{Beginning of business activity (operation)} \\
\text{Certificate} &= \text{CoI (Incorporation)} \cup \text{CoCB (Commencement)}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Parameter | Incorporation | Commencement |
|---|---|---|
| **Triggered by** | Filing of MOA + AOA | Filing of INC-21 |
| **Section** | Section 7 | Section 11 |
| **Form** | SPICe+ | INC-21 |
| **Certificate** | Certificate of Incorporation | Certificate of Commencement of Business |
| **Public Company** | Required | Required |
| **Private Company** | Required | NOT Required |
| **Time Available** | On ROC approval | Within 180 days of incorporation |
| **Legal Effect** | Company becomes a person | Company starts business |

### 3.4 Statutory Timeline Calculation

For KTU numerical/short problems, students should remember the key statutory periods:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{Name Reservation}} &= 20 \text{ days (extendable by } 60 \text{ days)} \\
T_{\text{Share Allotment}} &= 60 \text{ days after money receipt} \\
T_{\text{Refund (if default)}} &= 75 \text{ days} \\
T_{\text{Commencement (Public)}} &= 180 \text{ days from Incorporation} \\
T_{\text{AGM (First)}} &= \text{Within } 9 \text{ months from closing of first FY}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Example for KTU:** A public company was incorporated on **1st January 2024**. By which date must it obtain the Certificate of Commencement of Business?

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Date of Incorporation} &= 01 \text{-Jan-2024} \\
\text{Statutory Period} &= 180 \text{ days} \\
\text{Deadline} &= 01 \text{-Jan-2024} + 180 \text{ days} \\
&= 30 \text{-Jun-2024 (assuming non-leap year)}
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart – Commencement Procedure for Public Company

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Promoters Plan Business] --> B[Apply for Name Reservation - RUN]
    B --> C{Name Approved?}
    C -- No --> B1[Re-apply or Appeal]
    C -- Yes --> D[File MOA and AOA via SPICe+]
    D --> E[ROC Issues Certificate of Incorporation - Sec 7]
    E --> F[Company Becomes Legal Entity]
    F --> G[Allot Shares within 60 Days]
    G --> H[Directors File DIR-8 Self-Declaration]
    H --> I[Verify No Public Invitation Made]
    I --> J[Verify No Default on Deposits]
    J --> K[File Declaration in Form INC-21 with ROC]
    K --> L{ROC Satisfied?}
    L -- No --> M[Rectify Deficiencies]
    M --> K
    L -- Yes --> N[ROC Issues Certificate of Commencement]
    N --> O[Company Begins Business Operations]
    O --> P[Can Borrow, Raise Public Funds, Enter Contracts]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Decision Tree – Does a Company Need a Certificate of Commencement?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Company Incorporated?] --> Type{Type of Company?}
    Type -- Private --> Pvt[Private Company]
    Type -- Public --> Pub[Public Company]
    Type -- Sec8 --> Sec[Section 8 Company]
    Type -- NBFC --> NBFC[NBFC]

    Pvt --> P1[Can Start Business Immediately]
    P1 --> P2[No Certificate Required]
    P2 --> End[Business Begins]

    Pub --> Q1{Section 8?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Sec
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Deemed Public?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Sec
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{NBFC?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> NBFC
    Q3 -- No --> Apply[File INC-21]
    Apply --> Cert[Get Certificate of Commencement]
    Cert --> End

    Sec --> S1[Provisions of Section 8 apply - Modified Rules]
    NBFC --> N1[Must obtain Commencement Certificate]
    N1 --> Cert
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Architecture – Statutory Compliance Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Compliance[Statutory Compliance Block]
        A[Section 7 - Incorporation] --> B[Section 10 - Effect of Incorporation]
        B --> C[Section 11 - Commencement Rules]
        C --> D[Section 149 - Minimum Directors and Members]
        D --> E[Section 152 - Director Appointment]
        E --> F[Section 164 - Director Disqualification]
    end

    subgraph Output[Output Documents]
        A1[Certificate of Incorporation]
        C1[Certificate of Commencement]
        D1[DIR-12 Filing]
        F1[DIR-8 Declaration]
    end

    A --> A1
    C --> C1
    D --> D1
    F --> F1
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Comparison Chart – Public vs. Private Company Commencement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Public Company Path] --> P1[Name RUN]
    P1 --> P2[MOA and AOA Filed]
    P2 --> P3[CoI Issued]
    P3 --> P4[Allot Shares]
    P4 --> P5[File INC-21]
    P5 --> P6[Get CoCB]
    P6 --> P7[Start Business]

    Q[Private Company Path] --> Q1[Name RUN]
    Q1 --> Q2[MOA and AOA Filed]
    Q2 --> Q3[CoI Issued]
    Q3 --> Q4[Start Business Immediately]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
*What is meant by Commencement of Business? Is it different from Incorporation?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Commencement of Business** refers to the stage at which a legally incorporated company starts its actual business operations and exercises its statutory powers like borrowing, entering contracts, and raising capital.

**Difference from Incorporation:**
- **Incorporation** is the legal birth of the company upon receipt of the Certificate of Incorporation under Section 7.
- **Commencement** is the operational start of business activities after fulfillment of statutory requirements under Section 11.

A company exists after incorporation but cannot legally begin operations (in case of public companies) without obtaining the Certificate of Commencement of Business. *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
*Whether a private limited company requires a Certificate of Commencement of Business? Justify.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**No**, a private limited company **does not require** a Certificate of Commencement of Business under the Companies Act, 2013.

**Justification:** Under **Section 11(1)** of the Act, a private company (other than deemed public, Section 8, or NBFC) is permitted to commence business **immediately after incorporation**. It is not required to file Form INC-21 with the Registrar of Companies. Hence, the certificate is **not mandatory** for private companies. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B – Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** – *[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper 2024]*

**Q. (a)** Explain the legal provisions relating to the commencement of business by a public company under the Companies Act, 2013. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What are the consequences of commencing business before obtaining the Certificate of Commencement of Business? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Legal Provisions for Public Company Commencement (7 Marks):**

Under **Section 11(1)** of the Companies Act, 2013:

1. A public company shall not commence any business or exercise any borrowing powers unless:
   - A declaration is filed with the Registrar of Companies (ROC).
   - The ROC certifies that all requirements of Section 11 are complied with.

2. **Mandatory Pre-conditions (Section 11(3)):**
   - Every share allotted as part of the capital has been paid up in cash to the extent of its face value plus any premium.
   - No invitation has been made to the public to subscribe for shares or debentures.
   - Every director has verified (Form DIR-8) that they are not disqualified under Section 164.
   - No money is liable to be repaid as deposits.

3. **Procedure:**
   - File **Form INC-21** with the ROC.
   - Declaration signed by a director and certified by a **Chartered Accountant (CA)** or **Company Secretary (CS)**.
