# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedural Analysis & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Step-by-Step Process of a Takeover Under SEBI (SAST) Regulations, 2011

This is the **core 14-mark question blueprint** in KTU examinations. Master the sequencing of events.

### Step 1: Pre-Acquisition Planning
The acquirer identifies a target and conducts a strategic fit analysis. The acquirer may appoint **merchant bankers**, **legal advisors**, and **valuation experts**. No public disclosure is required at this stage.

> *Intuition:* Like planning a major purchase — research the house, the neighborhood, and the price history before making an offer.

### Step 2: Threshold Approach
The acquirer acquires shares (typically up to 24.99%) in the open market or through block deals. They must ensure the **25% threshold is not breached** without following the takeover code.

### Step 3: Triggering Event
The acquirer either:
- (a) Crosses the **25% threshold**, OR
- (b) Already holds 25%–75% and acquires an additional **5%** in a financial year (creeping), OR
- (c) Triggers a **change of control**.

### Step 4: Public Announcement (PA)
Within **2 working days** of triggering, the acquirer must make a **Public Announcement** of the open offer in:
- All editions of one English national daily
- All editions of one Hindi national daily
- One regional language daily of the place where the stock exchange is located
- The SEBI website and the stock exchange website

> *Intuition:* Like filing a public notice before holding a public auction.

### Step 5: Filing of Detailed Public Statement (DPS)
Within **5 working days** of the Public Announcement, the acquirer files a **Detailed Public Statement** with SEBI, the target company, and all stock exchanges where the target's shares are listed.

### Step 6: Board Recommendation
The **board of directors of the target company** is mandated (under Regulation 26) to provide a **reasoned recommendation** on the open offer within a stipulated period (currently 5 working days from receipt of the Detailed Public Statement).

### Step 7: Open Offer Window
The open offer remains open for a **minimum of 10 working days**. Shareholders can tender their shares during this window.

### Step 8: Settlement
Shares tendered by shareholders are acquired, and payment is made. The acquirer then files a report with SEBI.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Friendly vs Hostile Takeover

| Parameter | Friendly Takeover | Hostile Takeover |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Board Approval** | Required and obtained | Bypassed |
| **Negotiation** | Direct, between boards | Public, via open offer |
| **Speed** | Slower (multiple approvals) | Faster (no negotiation delay) |
| **Defensive Tactics** | Rarely needed | Aggressively used |
| **Premium Offered** | Usually lower | Usually higher (to attract shareholders) |
| **Outcome for Shareholders** | Fair price negotiated | Premium price, but subject to market volatility |
| **Outcome for Management** | Retained or gracefully exited | Often replaced |
| **Example Mechanism** | Scheme of Arrangement under Companies Act | Direct Open Offer bypassing board |

## 3.3 Numerical Illustration: Computing the Open Offer Price

**Problem Setup:**
- Acquirer purchases **24% of voting shares** at ₹500 per share on 1st January 2024.
- Later purchases **additional 2%** on 15th March 2024 at ₹540 per share (this triggers the takeover code, since holding rises from 24% to 26%).
- **52-week high price** (prior to PA): ₹560.
- **60-day VWAMP** prior to PA: ₹510.
- Public shareholders hold **65%** of the company.

**Step 1: Identify the Negotiated Price**

$$\text{Negotiated Price} = ₹540 \text{ per share (price paid for triggering acquisition)}$$

**Step 2: Identify the 52-Week High**

$$\text{52-Week High} = ₹560 \text{ per share}$$

**Step 3: Identify the 60-Day VWAMP**

$$\text{60-Day VWAMP} = ₹510 \text{ per share}$$

**Step 4: Apply the Maximum Rule**

$$\text{Offer Price} = \max(₹540, ₹560, ₹510)$$

$$\boxed{\text{Offer Price} = ₹560 \text{ per share}}$$

**Step 5: Calculate Total Open Offer Liability**

The acquirer must offer to buy a minimum of **26% of the public shareholding**.

$$\text{Public Shareholding} = 65\%$$

$$\text{Minimum Shares to be Acquired} = 26\% \times 65\% = 16.9\% \text{ of total shares}$$

Assuming total shares = **10 lakh**:

$$\text{Minimum Shares Required} = 16.9\% \times 10{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}69{,}000 \text{ shares}$$

$$\text{Minimum Cash Outlay} = 1{,}69{,}000 \times ₹560 = ₹9{,}46{,}40{,}000$$

$$\boxed{\text{Minimum Open Offer Liability} = ₹9.46 \text{ Crore (approx.)}}$$

## 3.4 Why Each Step Matters — The "Why" Behind the Code

- **Why 2 working days for PA?** Speed of information dissemination is critical; a delay could allow insider trading.
- **Why board recommendation?** Independent directors safeguard minority shareholder interests.
- **Why 60-day VWAMP?** Removes the impact of short-term price manipulation by the acquirer.
- **Why 26%?** SEBI determined that acquiring 26% gives the acquirer de facto control even with dispersed holdings.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define "Takeover" as per the SEBI (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011. List the three triggering events that mandate a public open offer.**

**Model Answer:**

A takeover refers to the acquisition of a substantial number of equity shares or voting rights in a listed company, resulting in the acquirer obtaining control over the management and affairs of the target company.

The three triggering events under the **SEBI (SAST) Regulations, 2011** are:
1. **Acquisition of 25% or more** of voting rights in the target.
2. **Acquisition of more than 5%** of voting rights in a financial year (creeping acquisition), when the acquirer already holds between 25% and the maximum permissible non-public shareholding.
3. **Change of control** of the target company, irrespective of the shareholding percentage.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Distinguish between a Friendly Takeover and a Hostile Takeover. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Friendly Takeover | Hostile Takeover |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition** | Acquirer negotiates directly with the target's board and obtains its approval. | Acquirer bypasses the target's board and directly approaches the public shareholders. |
| **Process** | Through a Scheme of Arrangement or negotiated share purchase. | Through a public open offer. |
| **Resistance** | Minimal, as board approves. | High; target deploys defensive tactics. |
| **Price** | Generally lower (no premium pressure). | Generally higher (to attract shareholders). |
| **Example** | Hindustan Unilever's acquisition of GSK Consumer Healthcare (2018). | The hostile bid by Nestlé for Cadbury in 2010. |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Internal Choice Pattern:** Students must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B.

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the procedural steps involved in a takeover under the **SEBI (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011**. Highlight the role of the **Public Announcement**, **Detailed Public Statement**, and the **Board's Recommendation**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the various **defensive strategies** that a target company may adopt to resist a hostile takeover bid. Provide Indian-context examples where relevant. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A (a):**

The procedure for a takeover under the **SEBI (SAST) Regulations, 2011** is as follows:

1. **Identification of Target:** The acquirer identifies a suitable target based on strategic, financial, and market considerations.

2. **Pre-Acquisition Preparation:** The acquirer appoints **merchant bankers, legal counsel, and valuers** to ensure regulatory compliance. Quiet market accumulation (up to 24.99%) is undertaken.

3. **Triggering Event:** The acquirer crosses any of the three thresholds:
   - **25% acquisition**,
   - **5% creeping acquisition** (in a financial year), or
   - **Change of control**.

4. **Public Announcement (PA):** Within **2 working days** of the triggering event, the acquirer must issue a Public Announcement in:
   - All editions of one English national daily,
   - All editions of one Hindi national daily,
   - A regional daily where the stock exchange is located, and
   - The SEBI and stock exchange websites.
   The PA discloses the acquirer's identity, offer price, and minimum offer size (26%).

5. **Detailed Public Statement (DPS):** Within **5 working days** of the PA, a Detailed Public Statement is filed with SEBI, the target, and stock exchanges. It contains the rationale, financing arrangement, and other detailed disclosures.

6. **Board Recommendation:** The board of the target, particularly the **independent directors**, must furnish a **reasoned recommendation** on the open offer to the shareholders within 5 working days of receiving the DPS. This ensures that minority shareholders are informed about the board's view on the fairness of the offer.

7. **Open Offer Period:** The offer remains open for a **minimum of 10 working days**. Shareholders can tender their shares.

8. **Settlement and Compliance:** Shares are settled, payment is made, and a post-offer report is filed with SEBI.

**Role of Each Disclosure:**
- **PA** ensures **immediate public awareness**.
- **DPS** provides **comprehensive information** for informed shareholder decision.
- **Board Recommendation** acts as a **safeguard for minority interests** by offering an independent view.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A (b):**

The principal defensive strategies are:

1. **Poison Pill:** Existing shareholders are issued rights to acquire additional shares at a discount if a hostile acquirer crosses a threshold, diluting the acquirer's stake. *Indian example:* The Colgate-Palmolive (India) anti-takeover provisions in its Articles of Association (a defensive structure often used in India).

2. **White Knight:** The target invites a friendly third party to make a counter-bid, displacing the hostile bidder. *Example:* When **Wipro** faced a hostile threat, it found a friendly counter-investor (white knight) to consolidate control.

3. **Black Knight:** A *unfriendly* counter-bidder, who displaces the original hostile bidder to gain control for themselves.

4. **Golden Parachute:** Generous severance packages for top management in the event of a takeover, making the acquisition financially unattractive.

5. **Crown Jewel Defense:** The target sells its most valuable division or asset to a third party, reducing its attractiveness to the hostile acquirer.

6. **Greenmail:** The target buys back its own shares from the hostile acquirer at a premium, in exchange for the acquirer agreeing to halt the bid.

7. **Pac-Man Defense:** The target company counter-acquires the hostile acquirer, reversing the strategic situation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often write only 2–3 defenses in a vague manner and lose 3–4 marks. For full 7 marks, you must **name at least 5 strategies**, **briefly explain each**, and **provide at least one example** (preferably Indian). Diagrams (like the Mermaid tree in SECTION 4) can earn extra marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** What is a **Conglomerate Takeover**? Explain its advantages and disadvantages from the perspective of the acquirer. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An acquirer purchases **24%** of a target company at **₹400 per share** on 1st February 2024. On 15th April 2024, it purchases an additional **2%** at **₹450 per share**, triggering the SEBI Takeover Code. The 52-week high is **₹470** and the 60-day VWAMP is **₹420**. Public shareholders hold **60%** of total 5 lakh shares. Calculate: (i) the offer price, and (ii) the minimum cash outlay for the open offer. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B (a):**

A **Conglomerate Takeover** is the acquisition of a target company that operates in an **unrelated business line** to that of the acquirer. For example, a software company acquiring a hospitality business.

**Advantages to the Acquirer:**

1. **Diversification of Risk:** The acquirer's revenue streams are no longer tied to a single industry, reducing the impact of industry-specific downturns.
2. **Portfolio Expansion:** Entry into high-growth unrelated sectors.
3. **Financial Synergies:** Use of surplus cash to acquire businesses with strong cash flows.
4. **Brand Extension:** Leveraging the corporate brand across diverse industries.
5. **Capital Allocation Efficiency:** Deployment of excess capital into profitable unrelated ventures.

**Disadvantages to the Acquirer:**

1. **Lack of Operational Synergy:** Unlike horizontal takeovers, there are no economies of scale.
2. **Management Challenges:** Managing diverse businesses requires wide-ranging expertise.
3. **Integration Difficulties:** Cultural and operational differences may lead to integration failures.
4. **Regulatory Scrutiny:** Often faces competition law and conglomerate-level oversight.
5. **Capital Stretch:** May overextend the balance sheet, leading to financial distress.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B (b):**

**Step 1: Identify the Three Prices**

- Negotiated Price (the price at which the trigger occurred) = **₹450 per share**
- 52-Week High Price = **₹470 per share**
- 60-Day VWAMP = **₹420 per share**

**Step 2: Apply the Maximum Rule**

$$\text{Offer Price} = \max(₹450, ₹470, ₹420)$$

$$\boxed{\text{Offer Price} = ₹470 \text{ per share}}$$

**Step 3: Determine the Minimum Open Offer Size**

The acquirer must offer to purchase a minimum of **26% of the public shareholding**.

$$\text{Public Shareholding} = 60\% \text{ of total shares}$$

$$\text{Minimum Acquisition Percentage} = 26\% \times 60\% = 15.6\% \text{ of total shares}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the Number of Shares**

$$\text{Total Shares} = 5{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{Minimum Shares to be Acquired} = 15.6\% \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 78{,}000 \text{ shares}$$

**Step 5: Calculate the Minimum Cash Outlay**

$$\text{Minimum Cash Outlay} = 78{,}000 \times ₹470$$

$$\text{Minimum Cash Outlay} = ₹3{,}66{,}60{,}000$$

$$\boxed{\text{Minimum Cash Outlay} = ₹3.666 \text{ Crore (approx.)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Valuation Mistake:** Students often take 26% of the **total shares** instead of 26% of the **public shareholding**. SEBI's 26% rule applies to the *public float*, not the entire share base. Always read the question carefully and apply the correct base.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Takeover** = Acquisition of control of a company by purchasing shares or assets.
- **SEBI (SAST) Regulations, 2011** is the governing law in India.
- **Three Triggering Thresholds:** 25% acquisition, 5% creeping acquisition, change of control.
- **Open Offer Size:** Minimum **26%** of public shareholding (not total shares).
- **Offer Price:** Highest of Negotiated Price, 52-Week High, 60-Day VWAMP.
- **Time Limits:** Public Announcement within 2 working days; Detailed Public Statement within 5 working days; offer open for minimum 10 working days.
- **Types:** Friendly vs Hostile; Horizontal vs Vertical vs Conglomerate.
- **Defensive Strategies:** Poison Pill, White Knight, Black Knight, Golden Parachute, Crown Jewel, Greenmail, Pac-Man.
- **Board's Role:** Independent directors must provide a reasoned recommendation (Regulation 26).
- **Exemptions:** Inter se transfers among PACs, allotments by the company, buybacks.
- **Common KTU Traps:** Confusing 25% (trigger) with 26% (offer size); applying 26% to total shares instead of public shareholding; mixing up horizontal and vertical takeovers.
